subtopic_detail: Foreign body aspiration - bronchoscopy decision
stem: An 18-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department after a sudden choking episode while eating peanuts 2 hours ago. His parents performed back blows at the scene and the choking resolved, but he has had a persistent cough since. He has no prior respiratory history and immunisations are up to date.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130, RR 34, SpO2 95% on room air, Temp 36.8 C
data_table.Examination: Mild subcostal recession. Right-sided wheeze on auscultation with decreased air entry at the right base. Left lung clear. No stridor at rest.
data_table.Investigations: CXR: hyperinflation of the right lung with mediastinal shift to the left on expiratory film. No radio-opaque foreign body seen.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Chest physiotherapy and observation for 24 hours
options.rationale: Chest physiotherapy is not indicated for a bronchial foreign body and risks distal migration or complete obstruction. It may be appropriate for mucus plugging but not a mechanical obstruction. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Aspiration)

options.text: Flexible nasendoscopy under local anaesthesia
options.rationale: Flexible nasendoscopy assesses supraglottic and glottic structures but cannot visualise or retrieve a distal bronchial foreign body. It is useful for laryngeal assessment but not the correct investigation here. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Aspiration)

options.text: CT chest with three-dimensional reconstruction
options.rationale: CT may confirm the location of a non-radio-opaque foreign body but delays definitive management. With a clear history and examination findings consistent with bronchial obstruction, proceeding directly to rigid bronchoscopy is preferred. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand

options.text: Rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Rigid bronchoscopy is the gold standard for both diagnosis and removal of an inhaled foreign body in a child. The combination of a witnessed choking episode, persistent unilateral wheeze, and expiratory air trapping on CXR makes the clinical probability high enough to proceed directly. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Aspiration)

options.text: Oral dexamethasone and reassessment in 4 hours
options.rationale: Dexamethasone reduces airway oedema in croup but does not address a mechanical bronchial obstruction. Delaying definitive management risks mucosal injury and granulation tissue formation around the foreign body. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Aspiration)

explanation.summary: Inhaled foreign bodies in toddlers most commonly lodge in the right main bronchus due to its wider diameter and more vertical orientation. The classic triad of sudden onset choking, unilateral wheeze, and air trapping on expiratory CXR has high positive predictive value. Rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia is both diagnostic and therapeutic, allowing safe retrieval with optical forceps. Organic foreign bodies such as peanuts cause a progressive inflammatory reaction and should be removed within 24 hours to minimise complications.
key_points: Right main bronchus is the most common site of foreign body lodgement in children due to its wider calibre and steeper angle
Expiratory CXR showing unilateral hyperinflation with mediastinal shift is the hallmark radiological finding
Rigid bronchoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic and should not be delayed for CT when clinical suspicion is high
Organic foreign bodies such as peanuts cause progressive mucosal inflammation and granulation within 24-48 hours
explanation.pearls: Peanuts and other organic foreign bodies provoke a lipoid pneumonia reaction that worsens after 24 hours - rigid bronchoscopy should ideally be performed within the first 24 hours of aspiration to reduce the risk of granulation tissue and mucosal damage.

sources.label: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand

subtopic_detail: Chronic suppurative otitis media in Aboriginal child
stem: A 6-year-old Aboriginal boy from a remote Northern Territory community presents to the visiting ear health clinic. His mother reports persistent discharge from his right ear for the past 5 months. He has had three courses of oral amoxicillin over this period with no improvement. His school teacher has raised concerns about inattention in class. He is otherwise well with no fever or systemic symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Right ear: mucopurulent discharge through a large central tympanic membrane perforation. Left ear: dull tympanic membrane with mild retraction. No mastoid tenderness bilaterally. No facial nerve palsy.
data_table.Investigations: Pure tone audiometry: 35 dB conductive hearing loss right ear, 20 dB conductive hearing loss left ear.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for a further 14 days
options.rationale: Systemic oral antibiotics alone have poor efficacy for CSOM. Multiple oral courses have already failed in this child. Australian guidelines prioritise topical therapy with aural toilet for uncomplicated CSOM. (Source: CARPA Standard Treatment Manual)

options.text: Topical ciprofloxacin drops with regular aural toilet
options.rationale: First-line treatment of CSOM in Australian Indigenous communities is dry mopping (aural toilet) combined with topical ciprofloxacin drops. This approach has superior efficacy to systemic antibiotics and avoids antimicrobial resistance. The CARPA manual recommends a minimum 2-week course with daily aural toilet. (Source: CARPA Standard Treatment Manual; eTG: Ear, Nose and Throat)

options.text: Urgent referral for tympanoplasty and mastoidectomy
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for CSOM that fails to respond to adequate topical therapy, or for complications such as cholesteatoma or mastoiditis. This child has not yet received appropriate topical treatment and does not have features of complicated disease. (Source: eTG: Ear, Nose and Throat)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone daily for 7 days in hospital
options.rationale: IV antibiotics are reserved for complicated CSOM with mastoiditis, intracranial extension, or systemic sepsis. This child has uncomplicated CSOM without danger signs, and hospitalisation is not warranted before trialling appropriate topical therapy. (Source: CARPA Standard Treatment Manual)

options.text: Grommet insertion under general anaesthesia bilaterally
options.rationale: Grommets are indicated for otitis media with effusion causing significant hearing loss, not for CSOM with an existing perforation. The right ear already has a perforation, making grommet insertion unnecessary on that side. (Source: eTG: Ear, Nose and Throat)

explanation.summary: Chronic suppurative otitis media is defined as persistent ear discharge through a tympanic membrane perforation for more than 6 weeks, though in high-prevalence populations treatment should begin earlier. It is disproportionately prevalent in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children, particularly in remote communities. The CARPA Standard Treatment Manual recommends regular dry mopping (aural toilet) combined with topical ciprofloxacin as first-line therapy. Hearing loss from CSOM can significantly impair language development and educational outcomes, making early effective treatment critical.
key_points: CSOM prevalence exceeds 10% in many remote Aboriginal communities, classified by WHO as requiring urgent attention
Topical ciprofloxacin with regular aural toilet is first-line, superior to systemic antibiotics alone
Oral antibiotics have poor penetration into chronically inflamed middle ear mucosa
Hearing loss from CSOM impacts language acquisition and school performance in affected children
Surgery is reserved for failure of adequate topical therapy or complicated disease
explanation.pearls: The CARPA manual specifies daily dry mopping before ciprofloxacin instillation for a minimum of 14 days - without adequate aural toilet, topical drops pool on debris and fail to contact the infected mucosa.

subtopic_detail: Retropharyngeal abscess - deep neck space infection
stem: A 3-year-old girl presents with a 3-day history of fever, progressive neck stiffness, and refusal to eat. She had an upper respiratory tract infection 1 week ago that appeared to resolve. Over the past 24 hours she has developed drooling and a muffled voice. She holds her neck in extension and resists flexion.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 160, RR 30, SpO2 97% on room air, Temp 39.4 C
data_table.Examination: Toxic-appearing child. Neck held in slight extension. Anterior cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally. Oropharynx: midline posterior pharyngeal wall bulge visible. No trismus.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22.4 x10^9/L (neutrophils 18.1), CRP 145 mg/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: IV flucloxacillin and metronidazole with urgent CT neck
options.rationale: A child with clinical features of retropharyngeal abscess requires IV antibiotics covering Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and anaerobes, plus urgent contrast-enhanced CT of the neck to confirm the diagnosis, delineate the extent of the collection, and determine whether surgical drainage is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Retropharyngeal Abscess; eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and outpatient follow-up
options.rationale: This child is toxic with high inflammatory markers and clinical signs of a deep neck space infection. Oral antibiotics and outpatient management are inadequate for a suspected retropharyngeal abscess, which carries risks of airway compromise and mediastinal extension. (Source: RCH CPG: Retropharyngeal Abscess)

options.text: Lateral neck X-ray and IV benzylpenicillin monotherapy
options.rationale: While a lateral neck X-ray may show prevertebral soft tissue widening, it cannot differentiate abscess from cellulitis or phlegmon. CT with contrast is the imaging modality of choice. Benzylpenicillin alone lacks adequate Staphylococcal and anaerobic cover. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Direct laryngoscopy and immediate incision and drainage
options.rationale: Surgical drainage may be required but should not proceed without imaging to delineate the collection. CT helps determine whether the collection is drainable or represents phlegmon that may respond to antibiotics alone. Blind incision risks vascular injury from the carotid sheath. (Source: RCH CPG: Retropharyngeal Abscess)

options.text: IV dexamethasone and nebulised adrenaline with observation
options.rationale: This management is appropriate for croup with upper airway obstruction, not for a deep neck space infection. While steroids may have an adjunctive role, they do not address the underlying infection and the child requires antibiotics, imaging, and likely surgical consultation. (Source: RCH CPG: Retropharyngeal Abscess)

explanation.summary: Retropharyngeal abscess typically occurs in children under 5 years due to the presence of retropharyngeal lymph nodes that involute by age 6. It often follows an upper respiratory infection or pharyngitis. The classic presentation includes fever, neck stiffness with extension preference, drooling, and a posterior pharyngeal bulge. Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck is essential to distinguish abscess (requiring drainage) from phlegmon (may respond to antibiotics alone). Complications include airway obstruction, mediastinitis, and internal jugular vein thrombosis.
key_points: Retropharyngeal abscess occurs predominantly in children under 5 due to the presence of retropharyngeal lymph nodes
Neck extension preference (as opposed to flexion in meningitis) is a key discriminating clinical sign
Contrast-enhanced CT is the imaging modality of choice to differentiate abscess from phlegmon
Empiric antibiotics must cover Streptococcus, Staphylococcus aureus, and oral anaerobes
explanation.pearls: Children with retropharyngeal abscess characteristically hold their neck in extension and resist flexion - the opposite of meningism - because flexion compresses the abscess against the posterior pharynx and worsens pain.

subtopic_detail: Primary nocturnal enuresis - management after alarm failure
stem: A 9-year-old boy presents with ongoing primary nocturnal enuresis. He wets the bed 5 nights per week and has never had a sustained dry period. He has no daytime symptoms, no constipation, and no urinary tract infections. A 3-month trial of an enuresis alarm was attempted with good compliance but produced no improvement. His urinalysis is normal and postvoid residual is negligible on ultrasound.

data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: specific gravity 1.020, no protein, no glucose, no leucocytes, no nitrites. Renal tract ultrasound: normal kidneys and bladder, postvoid residual < 10 mL.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat the enuresis alarm trial for a further 3 months
options.rationale: An adequate alarm trial is 6-8 weeks with good compliance. This child has had a 3-month trial with no improvement, indicating alarm therapy failure. Repeating the same intervention is unlikely to succeed and delays alternative treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Nocturnal Enuresis)

options.text: Fluid restriction after 5pm with scheduled overnight waking
options.rationale: Evening fluid restriction alone has limited evidence for efficacy and scheduled overnight waking is disruptive without evidence of sustained benefit. After alarm failure, pharmacotherapy is the recommended next step. (Source: NICE: Nocturnal Enuresis)

options.text: Oral desmopressin melt at bedtime for an initial 3-month trial
options.rationale: After alarm therapy failure, desmopressin (DDAVP) is the second-line treatment. The sublingual melt formulation is preferred in children. A 3-month trial with structured withdrawal (gradual dose reduction) is recommended. Response is expected within 2 weeks. Fluid restriction 1 hour before to 8 hours after dosing is essential to avoid hyponatraemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Nocturnal Enuresis; AMH Children's Dosing Companion)

options.text: Oral oxybutynin for overactive bladder with enuresis component
options.rationale: Oxybutynin is indicated for enuresis with daytime overactive bladder symptoms such as urgency, frequency, or daytime wetting. This child has no daytime symptoms, making anticholinergic therapy inappropriate as monotherapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Nocturnal Enuresis)

options.text: Referral for urodynamic studies and specialist urology review
options.rationale: Urodynamic studies are reserved for children with complex lower urinary tract symptoms, daytime incontinence unresponsive to treatment, or suspected neuropathic bladder. A child with isolated monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis and normal investigations does not require urodynamics at this stage. (Source: RCH CPG: Nocturnal Enuresis)

explanation.summary: Primary nocturnal enuresis is involuntary night-time bedwetting in a child over 5 years who has never achieved sustained dryness. It is monosymptomatic when there are no daytime lower urinary tract symptoms. First-line treatment is an enuresis alarm, which has the highest long-term cure rate. When alarm therapy fails after an adequate trial, desmopressin is recommended as second-line. Desmopressin reduces nocturnal urine production and has rapid onset, but relapse rates are higher than alarm therapy once discontinued.
key_points: Enuresis alarm is first-line with a 70% success rate but requires 6-8 weeks of consistent use
Desmopressin is second-line after alarm failure, with response expected within 1-2 weeks
Structured withdrawal of desmopressin over 2-4 weeks reduces relapse rates compared to abrupt cessation
Fluid restriction from 1 hour before to 8 hours after desmopressin is mandatory to prevent hyponatraemia
Oxybutynin is reserved for enuresis with daytime overactive bladder symptoms
explanation.pearls: Desmopressin-associated hyponatraemia is rare but potentially fatal - fluid intake must be restricted from 1 hour before dosing to 8 hours after, and the drug must be withheld during intercurrent illnesses with vomiting or excessive fluid intake.

subtopic_detail: Delayed puberty - constitutional vs hypogonadotropic
stem: A 15-year-old boy is referred by his GP for absent pubertal development. He has no pubic hair, testicular volumes are 2 mL bilaterally, and he has not had a growth spurt. He is at the 3rd centile for height. His father reports that he himself was a late developer who did not shave until age 18. The boy is otherwise well with no chronic illness, headaches, or visual disturbance. He has a normal sense of smell.

data_table.Vital signs: Height 152 cm (3rd centile), weight 43 kg (10th centile), BMI 18.6
data_table.Investigations: LH 1.2 IU/L, FSH 1.8 IU/L, testosterone 0.8 nmol/L, prolactin 180 mIU/L (normal), IGF-1 low-normal for age, bone age 12.5 years (chronological age 15 years)

lead_in: What is the single most useful investigation to distinguish constitutional delay from hypogonadotropic hypogonadism?

options.text: Karyotype analysis for chromosomal abnormalities
options.rationale: Karyotype is useful for suspected Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY), which presents with hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (elevated LH/FSH). This boy has low gonadotrophins, making karyotype less discriminating at this stage. (Source: RCH CPG: Delayed Puberty)

options.text: GnRH stimulation test with serial LH measurements
options.rationale: A GnRH stimulation test can assess pituitary LH reserve but has poor sensitivity in distinguishing constitutional delay from hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, as both conditions show blunted responses before puberty. It is no longer recommended as a first-line discriminating test. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Serum inhibin B and anti-Mullerian hormone levels
options.rationale: Inhibin B and AMH reflect Sertoli cell function and may be low in both conditions in prepubertal boys. While emerging evidence supports their use, they are not yet established as the primary discriminating investigation in Australian guidelines. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Serum kisspeptin and neurokinin B measurement
options.rationale: Kisspeptin and neurokinin B are research biomarkers for GnRH neuronal function and are not available as routine clinical assays. They cannot be used in standard clinical practice to discriminate these conditions. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Delayed Puberty)

options.text: MRI brain with pituitary protocol and olfactory assessment
options.rationale: MRI with pituitary protocol evaluates for structural causes of hypogonadotropic hypogonadism including pituitary hypoplasia, absent pituitary stalk, and olfactory bulb aplasia (Kallmann syndrome). In a 15-year-old with prepubertal gonadotrophins and no pubertal onset, MRI is the single most informative investigation to identify treatable pathology versus reassuring constitutional delay. Normal olfactory bulbs and pituitary structure support constitutional delay. (Source: RCH CPG: Delayed Puberty; Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Distinguishing constitutional delay of growth and puberty from hypogonadotropic hypogonadism is one of the most challenging diagnostic problems in paediatric endocrinology. Both conditions present with low gonadotrophins and delayed bone age. Constitutional delay is supported by a family history of late puberty and will resolve spontaneously. MRI brain with pituitary protocol can identify structural causes such as Kallmann syndrome (olfactory bulb aplasia), pituitary hypoplasia, or tumours. When imaging is normal and family history is supportive, a therapeutic trial of low-dose testosterone for 3-6 months can help - spontaneous progression after withdrawal favours constitutional delay.
key_points: Constitutional delay and hypogonadotropic hypogonadism both present with low LH, low FSH, and delayed bone age
MRI with pituitary protocol and olfactory bulb assessment is the most informative single investigation
Kallmann syndrome combines hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with anosmia and absent olfactory bulbs on MRI
A bone age delay of 2 or more years is typical of constitutional delay
Therapeutic trial of low-dose testosterone for 3-6 months with reassessment helps confirm the diagnosis
explanation.pearls: If a 15-year-old boy has testicular volumes below 4 mL and LH remains below 2 IU/L, the probability of spontaneous pubertal onset within 12 months is low - MRI should be obtained before reassuring as constitutional delay.

subtopic_detail: Acute scrotal pain - testicular torsion exploration
stem: A 14-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with sudden onset severe left scrotal pain that began 3 hours ago during a basketball game. He has vomited twice and the pain radiates to the left groin. He denies urinary symptoms, penile discharge, or recent trauma. He has no prior episodes.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, BP 125/75 mmHg, Temp 37.1 C
data_table.Examination: Left testis high-riding and transversely oriented. Absent cremasteric reflex on the left. Exquisitely tender left hemiscrotum with a firm, swollen testis. Right testis normal. No erythema of scrotal skin. Negative Prehn sign.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: no leucocytes, no nitrites, no blood.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Urgent scrotal Doppler ultrasound to confirm diagnosis
options.rationale: When clinical features are highly suggestive of testicular torsion, ultrasound should not delay surgical exploration. Testicular salvage rates drop significantly after 6 hours of ischaemia, and the time spent obtaining imaging may cross this threshold. Ultrasound sensitivity for torsion is also not 100%. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Scrotal Pain)

options.text: Immediate surgical exploration and orchidopexy
options.rationale: This presentation has the classic features of testicular torsion: sudden severe pain, high-riding transversely oriented testis, absent cremasteric reflex, and negative Prehn sign. With 3 hours of symptom duration, immediate surgical exploration offers the best chance of testicular salvage. Bilateral orchidopexy (fixation of both testes) is performed at the same time. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Scrotal Pain; Therapeutic Guidelines: Surgery)

options.text: Manual detorsion attempt with analgesia in the ED
options.rationale: Manual detorsion (lateral rotation of the affected testis - 'open the book') can be attempted as a temporising measure but is not definitive management. Even if successful, surgical orchidopexy is still required to prevent recurrence. With theatre access available within the salvage window, proceeding directly to exploration is preferred. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Scrotal Pain)

options.text: IV antibiotics for presumed epididymo-orchitis
options.rationale: Epididymo-orchitis is uncommon in prepubertal and early-adolescent boys without urinary tract abnormalities. The clinical features here (sudden onset, high-riding testis, absent cremasteric reflex, clear urinalysis) strongly favour torsion over infection. Treating for infection delays surgical exploration. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Scrotal Pain)

options.text: Analgesia and repeat examination in 2 hours
options.rationale: Expectant management is dangerous in suspected testicular torsion. Testicular salvage rates are approximately 90% within 6 hours but drop to less than 10% after 24 hours. A 2-hour delay could push this patient beyond the optimal salvage window. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Scrotal Pain)

explanation.summary: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency with a bimodal peak in neonates and adolescents (12-18 years). The pathognomonic finding is a high-riding, transversely oriented testis with an absent cremasteric reflex. When clinical suspicion is high, immediate surgical exploration should not be delayed for imaging. Testicular salvage rates are strongly time-dependent, with near-complete salvage within 6 hours declining rapidly thereafter. Bilateral orchidopexy is performed during exploration to prevent contralateral torsion.
key_points: Absent cremasteric reflex has the highest sensitivity (99%) among individual clinical signs for testicular torsion
Testicular salvage rate is approximately 90% within 6 hours, 50% at 12 hours, and less than 10% after 24 hours
Ultrasound should not delay exploration when clinical probability of torsion is high
Bilateral orchidopexy is performed at the time of exploration regardless of whether the testis is viable
Torsion of a testicular appendage (hydatid of Morgagni) is the most common differential and shows a blue dot sign
explanation.pearls: The cremasteric reflex is elicited by stroking the inner thigh and observing ipsilateral testicular elevation - its absence in a boy with acute scrotal pain has a sensitivity of 99% for testicular torsion and should prompt immediate surgical referral without imaging.

subtopic_detail: Appendicitis in young child - atypical presentation
stem: A 3-year-old girl presents with a 2-day history of irritability, poor oral intake, and intermittent crying with her legs drawn up. She has had two episodes of loose stool and a low-grade fever. Her parents initially attributed the symptoms to gastroenteritis. She has no prior abdominal surgery. On examination she is distressed with handling and localises pain to the right lower quadrant when the examiner gently presses on the abdomen.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 155, RR 28, Temp 38.3 C, SpO2 99%
data_table.Examination: Rigid abdomen with guarding in the right iliac fossa and suprapubic region. Decreased bowel sounds. No palpable mass. Rovsing sign positive.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 19.2 x10^9/L (neutrophils 15.6), CRP 85 mg/L. Urinalysis: trace leucocytes, no nitrites.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Oral rehydration and observation for viral gastroenteritis
options.rationale: While diarrhoea can occur in appendicitis (especially with a pelvic appendix irritating the rectum), the combination of localised right iliac fossa guarding, peritoneal signs, elevated inflammatory markers, and prolonged symptoms in a 3-year-old warrants surgical assessment rather than expectant management for gastroenteritis. (Source: RCH CPG: Appendicitis)

options.text: Abdominal X-ray to assess for obstruction or faecolith
options.rationale: Abdominal X-ray has poor sensitivity and specificity for appendicitis and is not recommended as the primary imaging modality. An appendicolith is seen in fewer than 10% of cases. Ultrasound is the preferred first-line imaging in children. (Source: RCH CPG: Appendicitis)

options.text: Ultrasound abdomen and urgent paediatric surgical review
options.rationale: Appendicitis in young children has a high perforation rate (up to 80% in children under 5) due to delayed and atypical presentations. Ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality in children and can demonstrate a non-compressible appendix greater than 6 mm, periappendiceal fluid, or abscess. Concurrent surgical review is essential given the high clinical suspicion and risk of perforation at this age. (Source: RCH CPG: Appendicitis; RACS Guidelines)

options.text: CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast for diagnosis
options.rationale: CT has high sensitivity for appendicitis but exposes the child to ionising radiation. In paediatric practice, ultrasound is the recommended first-line imaging modality. CT is reserved for cases where ultrasound is inconclusive and clinical suspicion remains high. (Source: RCH CPG: Appendicitis)

options.text: IV antibiotics alone with plan for interval appendicectomy
options.rationale: Antibiotic-only management with interval appendicectomy may be appropriate for an appendicular mass or contained perforation, but the decision requires imaging first. In a child with peritonism, delaying surgical assessment and imaging to trial antibiotics alone risks progression to generalised peritonitis. (Source: RACS Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Appendicitis in children under 5 years is frequently misdiagnosed because the presentation is often atypical - irritability, anorexia, loose stools, and diffuse tenderness may mimic gastroenteritis. The perforation rate in this age group approaches 80% due to diagnostic delay and a thinner appendiceal wall with a less developed omentum that poorly walls off infection. Ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality in paediatric appendicitis, with a sensitivity of 85-95% in experienced hands. Early surgical review is critical when clinical suspicion is high.
key_points: Perforation rate in appendicitis approaches 80% in children under 5 years due to atypical presentation and diagnostic delay
Diarrhoea occurs in up to 33% of children with appendicitis, particularly with a pelvic appendix
Ultrasound is first-line imaging - a non-compressible appendix greater than 6 mm diameter is diagnostic
CT should be reserved for inconclusive ultrasound with ongoing clinical suspicion
Trace leucocytes on urinalysis can occur from an inflamed appendix adjacent to the bladder or ureter
explanation.pearls: In children under 5 with appendicitis, the perforation rate at presentation is 50-80% compared with 10-20% in older children - the thin-walled appendix and poorly developed omentum allow rapid progression from inflammation to perforation within 24-36 hours of symptom onset.

subtopic_detail: Neuroblastoma - paraneoplastic presentation
stem: A 22-month-old girl presents with a 3-week history of progressive unsteadiness when walking and involuntary jerking movements of her limbs. Her parents describe chaotic eye movements that have worsened over the past week. She has become increasingly irritable and her sleep is disturbed. Prior to this she was developing normally. There is no history of recent infection or travel.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 120, RR 24, SpO2 99%, Temp 36.9 C
data_table.Examination: Alert but irritable child. Chaotic multidirectional conjugate saccadic eye movements (opsoclonus). Myoclonic jerks of the trunk and limbs. Truncal ataxia - unable to sit unsupported. Palpable firm non-tender 3 cm mass in the left flank. No hepatomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: FBC and UEC normal. LDH 320 U/L (mildly elevated).

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial investigation to identify the underlying cause?

options.text: Urine catecholamines and abdominal ultrasound
options.rationale: Opsoclonus-myoclonus-ataxia syndrome in a toddler with a palpable abdominal mass is strongly suggestive of a neural crest tumour, most commonly neuroblastoma. Spot urine for vanillylmandelic acid (VMA) and homovanillic acid (HVA) is the appropriate first-line biochemical test, and abdominal ultrasound will characterise the mass. Up to 50% of children with opsoclonus-myoclonus have an underlying neuroblastoma. (Source: Children's Cancer and Leukaemia Group; RCH CPG: Abdominal Mass)

options.text: MRI brain and lumbar puncture for CSF analysis
options.rationale: MRI brain may eventually be performed to exclude posterior fossa pathology, but the combination of opsoclonus-myoclonus with a palpable abdominal mass makes a paraneoplastic syndrome from neuroblastoma the most likely diagnosis. Investigating the mass is the priority. Lumbar puncture may show CSF lymphocytosis but is not specific. (Source: Children's Cancer and Leukaemia Group)

options.text: EEG with prolonged monitoring for epileptic activity
options.rationale: Opsoclonus-myoclonus is not an epileptic phenomenon and EEG will typically be normal or show non-specific slowing. The chaotic saccadic eye movements and myoclonus are mediated by anti-neuronal antibodies, not cortical epileptic discharges. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Opsoclonus-Myoclonus)

options.text: CT chest and abdomen with IV contrast and biopsy
options.rationale: While CT may be needed for staging, it is not the appropriate first-line investigation in a young child. Ultrasound avoids radiation and can characterise the mass adequately as an initial step. Urine catecholamines provide a non-invasive biochemical marker that should precede biopsy. (Source: Children's Cancer and Leukaemia Group)

options.text: Anti-neuronal antibody panel and serum ferritin
options.rationale: Anti-neuronal antibodies are associated with opsoclonus-myoclonus but their absence does not exclude neuroblastoma. The priority is to identify and characterise the abdominal mass with non-invasive testing. Antibody panels may be sent but are not the most appropriate initial investigation. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Opsoclonus-Myoclonus)

explanation.summary: Opsoclonus-myoclonus-ataxia (OMA) syndrome in a child under 3 years should always prompt investigation for neuroblastoma. Approximately 50% of paediatric OMA cases are paraneoplastic, with the remainder being post-infectious or idiopathic. Neuroblastoma associated with OMA tends to be lower-stage and better-differentiated, paradoxically carrying a better oncological prognosis than neuroblastoma without OMA. However, the neurological outcome is often poor with cognitive and motor sequelae persisting despite tumour treatment. Treatment involves tumour resection and immunomodulatory therapy.
key_points: Opsoclonus-myoclonus-ataxia syndrome in a toddler is paraneoplastic until proven otherwise
Urine VMA and HVA are elevated in approximately 90% of neuroblastomas
Neuroblastoma with OMA is typically lower-stage with favourable biology (MYCN non-amplified)
Neurological prognosis is often poor despite good oncological outcomes, with 70-80% having residual cognitive or motor deficits
Immunomodulatory treatment with ACTH or IVIG is required in addition to tumour resection
explanation.pearls: Neuroblastoma-associated opsoclonus-myoclonus paradoxically carries a better tumour prognosis (5-year survival over 90%) but a worse neurological prognosis than other neuroblastomas - up to 80% of children have persistent cognitive or motor impairment despite complete tumour remission.

subtopic_detail: Acute osteomyelitis - IV to oral switch criteria
stem: A 7-year-old boy was admitted 5 days ago with acute haematogenous osteomyelitis of the left distal tibia, confirmed on MRI. Blood cultures grew methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus. He has been receiving IV flucloxacillin since admission. His pain has improved significantly and he is now mobilising with crutches. He is tolerating oral fluids and food well.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, Temp 36.8 C (afebrile for 48 hours)
data_table.Investigations: Day 1: CRP 145 mg/L, WCC 18.2 x10^9/L. Day 5 (today): CRP 32 mg/L (trending down), WCC 10.4 x10^9/L. Blood cultures day 3: no growth (repeat).

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in antibiotic management?

options.text: Continue IV flucloxacillin for a total of 14 days
options.rationale: Prolonged IV therapy was traditional practice but contemporary evidence supports early switch to oral antibiotics in uncomplicated osteomyelitis when specific clinical and biochemical criteria are met. Extending IV therapy increases the risk of line-related complications without improving outcomes. (Source: RCH CPG: Osteomyelitis; Peltola et al, NEJM 2014)

options.text: Continue IV flucloxacillin until CRP normalises completely
options.rationale: Waiting for complete CRP normalisation before switching to oral therapy is unnecessary. The key criterion is a downward CRP trend, not normalisation. CRP may take 2-3 weeks to fully normalise even with successful treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Osteomyelitis)

options.text: Switch to oral cefalexin with CRP monitoring as outpatient
options.rationale: This child meets all criteria for IV-to-oral switch: afebrile for more than 48 hours, CRP trending down by more than 50%, clinical improvement with reduced pain and mobilisation, organism identified and sensitive, and able to tolerate oral medication. High-dose oral cefalexin (or flucloxacillin) is appropriate for MSSA osteomyelitis with outpatient CRP monitoring at 1-2 week intervals. (Source: RCH CPG: Osteomyelitis; eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Switch to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for 3 weeks total
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate provides broader coverage than needed for confirmed MSSA osteomyelitis. Directed therapy with a narrow-spectrum agent (cefalexin or flucloxacillin) targeting the known organism is preferred to reduce unnecessary broad-spectrum exposure. Total treatment duration is typically 3-4 weeks. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Insert a PICC line for outpatient parenteral therapy
options.rationale: Outpatient parenteral antibiotic therapy (OPAT) via PICC line is reserved for cases where oral therapy is not feasible (unable to tolerate oral, resistant organism, complicated infection). This child has uncomplicated MSSA osteomyelitis with all switch criteria met, making a PICC line an unnecessary escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Osteomyelitis)

explanation.summary: Contemporary paediatric osteomyelitis management has shifted from prolonged IV therapy to early oral switch based on landmark trials showing equivalent outcomes. The RCH CPG and international evidence support switching to oral antibiotics after a minimum of 3 days IV therapy when the child is afebrile for 48 hours, CRP is trending down by more than 50%, the organism is identified and sensitive, the child is clinically improving, and oral intake is reliable. Total antibiotic duration for uncomplicated osteomyelitis is typically 3-4 weeks.
key_points: IV-to-oral switch criteria: afebrile 48 hours, CRP trending down more than 50%, organism known, clinical improvement, tolerating oral
High-dose oral cefalexin or flucloxacillin is appropriate for MSSA osteomyelitis after IV-to-oral switch
Total antibiotic duration for uncomplicated osteomyelitis is 3-4 weeks, not 6 weeks as historically taught
CRP should be monitored weekly after discharge - a secondary rise suggests treatment failure or complication
PICC lines and prolonged IV therapy increase line infection risk without improving outcomes in uncomplicated cases
explanation.pearls: The Peltola and OVIVA trials demonstrated that oral antibiotics are non-inferior to prolonged IV therapy for uncomplicated osteomyelitis - Australian guidelines now recommend oral switch after 3-5 days IV when CRP has fallen by more than 50% from peak.

subtopic_detail: Supracondylar fracture - neurovascular assessment
stem: A 5-year-old girl falls from monkey bars onto an outstretched hand. She presents with a swollen, painful left elbow held in flexion. On assessment she cannot make an 'OK' sign with her left hand - she is unable to flex the interphalangeal joint of her thumb or the distal interphalangeal joint of her index finger. She has intact radial and ulnar nerve function. Her radial pulse is palpable and capillary refill is 2 seconds in all fingers.

data_table.Examination: Marked swelling of the left elbow with ecchymosis anteriorly. Passive extension limited by pain. Unable to flex IPJ of thumb or DIPJ of index finger (anterior interosseous nerve). Intact thumb and finger extension, intact finger abduction and adduction. Radial pulse present, warm hand.
data_table.Investigations: X-ray left elbow: Gartland type III extension-type supracondylar fracture with posterior displacement of the distal fragment. Anterior humeral line does not intersect the capitellum.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of this fracture?

options.text: Above-elbow backslab and orthopaedic review in 24 hours
options.rationale: A Gartland III supracondylar fracture is inherently unstable with displaced fragments. Backslab immobilisation and delayed review risks further displacement, worsening neurovascular compromise, and compartment syndrome. Urgent operative fixation is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Supracondylar Fractures)

options.text: Closed reduction and percutaneous K-wire fixation urgently
options.rationale: Closed reduction and K-wire fixation is the standard treatment for most Gartland III supracondylar fractures. However, this child has an anterior interosseous nerve (AIN) palsy suggesting the nerve may be trapped or tethered at the fracture site. If the AIN palsy does not resolve after closed reduction, open exploration may be required. While this option is partially correct, the nerve injury changes the operative approach. (Source: RCH CPG: Supracondylar Fractures)

options.text: Collar and cuff sling with urgent nerve conduction studies
options.rationale: Nerve conduction studies are not indicated acutely in the setting of a displaced fracture with nerve palsy. The nerve injury is a neuropraxia or traction injury from the fracture displacement, and the priority is fracture reduction. NCS may have a role weeks later if recovery does not occur. (Source: RCH CPG: Supracondylar Fractures)

options.text: Fasciotomy of the forearm for suspected compartment syndrome
options.rationale: Compartment syndrome causes pain with passive extension of the fingers, a tense swollen forearm, and diminished pulses - not an isolated AIN palsy with normal vascular status. The AIN deficit here is from nerve traction or entrapment at the fracture site, not forearm compartment pressure. (Source: RCH CPG: Supracondylar Fractures)

options.text: Urgent closed reduction with K-wires and AIN reassessment
options.rationale: The standard management of a Gartland III supracondylar fracture with AIN palsy is urgent closed reduction and percutaneous K-wire fixation, followed by reassessment of AIN function post-operatively. Most AIN palsies (over 90%) associated with supracondylar fractures are neuropraxias that resolve spontaneously after fracture reduction. Open exploration is only required if the nerve palsy develops new or worsens after reduction. (Source: RCH CPG: Supracondylar Fractures; BOAST: Supracondylar Fractures in Children)

explanation.summary: Supracondylar fractures are the most common elbow fracture in children aged 3-8 years. Gartland III fractures are completely displaced and require urgent operative fixation with closed reduction and percutaneous K-wires. The anterior interosseous nerve is the most commonly injured nerve in extension-type supracondylar fractures, presenting with inability to flex the IPJ of the thumb and DIPJ of the index finger (unable to make an 'OK' sign). Over 90% of nerve palsies associated with these fractures are neuropraxias that resolve spontaneously after fracture reduction within 2-3 months. Post-operative neurovascular reassessment is essential.
key_points: AIN palsy is the most common nerve injury in extension-type supracondylar fractures
AIN palsy manifests as inability to flex the IPJ of the thumb and DIPJ of the index finger
Over 90% of nerve palsies from supracondylar fractures resolve after fracture reduction
Open nerve exploration is indicated only if a new nerve deficit appears or worsens after reduction
The anterior humeral line should bisect the middle third of the capitellum on a lateral X-ray
explanation.pearls: The anterior interosseous nerve is a pure motor branch of the median nerve - its palsy produces no sensory loss, only inability to flex the thumb IPJ and index finger DIPJ, tested by asking the child to make an 'OK' sign.

subtopic_detail: Neonatal seizures - metabolic workup and pyridoxine trial
stem: A 3-day-old term neonate develops recurrent multifocal clonic seizures that are refractory to two loading doses of phenobarbitone. The pregnancy was uncomplicated, delivery was normal, and Apgar scores were 9 at 1 minute and 9 at 5 minutes. There were no risk factors for sepsis. The baby was well for the first 48 hours before seizures began.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 145, RR 42, SpO2 98%, Temp 36.9 C
data_table.Investigations: BSL 3.8 mmol/L. Corrected calcium 2.35 mmol/L. Magnesium 0.85 mmol/L. Sodium 139 mmol/L. FBC: WCC 12.4 x10^9/L, platelets 280 x10^9/L. Blood culture: pending. LP: clear CSF, WCC 2, protein 0.8 g/L, glucose 2.6 mmol/L. Ammonia 45 micromol/L (normal). Cranial ultrasound: normal.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Empiric IV aciclovir for possible neonatal HSV encephalitis
options.rationale: HSV encephalitis typically presents with fever, lethargy, and CSF pleocytosis. This neonate has normal CSF cell count, no fever, and no risk factors. While HSV PCR should be sent, the normal CSF makes HSV less likely and does not explain phenobarbitone-refractory seizures. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Seizures)

options.text: IV pyridoxine trial with concurrent EEG monitoring
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone-refractory neonatal seizures with normal metabolic screen and normal neuroimaging should prompt a trial of IV pyridoxine (100 mg) with concurrent EEG monitoring. Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy is a treatable cause of refractory neonatal seizures caused by ALDH7A1 mutation. Dramatic cessation of seizure activity during the pyridoxine infusion is diagnostic. Concurrent EEG is essential to document the electrographic response. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Seizures; ILAE Neonatal Seizure Guidelines)

options.text: Third loading dose of phenobarbitone to maximum level
options.rationale: A third loading dose of phenobarbitone risks respiratory depression and cardiovascular instability. After two adequate loading doses, further phenobarbitone is unlikely to be effective and alternative causes of refractory seizures should be investigated and treated. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Seizures)

options.text: MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging sequences
options.rationale: MRI brain is indicated to evaluate for structural causes and ischaemic injury, but cranial ultrasound is already normal and the clinical picture (refractory seizures with normal metabolic screen in a well neonate) prioritises a treatable metabolic cause. MRI can be arranged after trialling pyridoxine. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Seizures)

options.text: Midazolam infusion for refractory status epilepticus
options.rationale: Midazolam infusion may be used for refractory neonatal seizures but should not be commenced before excluding treatable metabolic causes. Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy will not respond to benzodiazepines, and missing this diagnosis has lifelong consequences as ongoing treatment with oral pyridoxine prevents seizures and developmental regression. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Seizures)

explanation.summary: Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy (PDE) is an autosomal recessive condition caused by ALDH7A1 mutations that impairs cerebral pyridoxal-5-phosphate metabolism. It typically presents with refractory neonatal seizures unresponsive to conventional anticonvulsants. The diagnostic trial involves administering 100 mg IV pyridoxine with EEG monitoring - seizure cessation within minutes is characteristic. Without lifelong pyridoxine supplementation, affected children develop severe intellectual disability. PDE should be considered in any neonate with seizures refractory to two anticonvulsants, even when standard metabolic investigations are normal.
key_points: Pyridoxine trial is indicated for neonatal seizures refractory to two anticonvulsants with a normal metabolic screen
IV pyridoxine 100 mg with concurrent EEG monitoring is the diagnostic test
Dramatic seizure cessation within minutes of pyridoxine infusion is characteristic of PDE
Alpha-aminoadipic semialdehyde (AASA) in urine and pipecolic acid in plasma are confirmatory biomarkers
Lifelong oral pyridoxine supplementation (15-30 mg/kg/day) is required to prevent recurrence
explanation.pearls: Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy due to ALDH7A1 mutation is confirmed by elevated urinary alpha-aminoadipic semialdehyde (AASA) - but the pyridoxine trial should never be delayed for biochemical results, as each hour of uncontrolled seizures worsens neurodevelopmental outcome.

subtopic_detail: TTN versus RDS discrimination in late preterm
stem: A male infant is born at 35+2 weeks gestation by elective caesarean section for breech presentation. No antenatal corticosteroids were administered. Within 30 minutes of birth he develops grunting, nasal flaring, and subcostal recession. He is placed on CPAP at 7 cmH2O with FiO2 0.35.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 160, RR 72, SpO2 91% on room air (pre-CPAP), 96% on CPAP 7/0.35, Temp 36.7 C
data_table.Examination: Moderate subcostal and intercostal recession. Bilateral fine inspiratory crackles. No focal wheeze.
data_table.Investigations: CXR at 2 hours: diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacification with air bronchograms and low lung volumes. Blood gas (capillary): pH 7.28, pCO2 52 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L, BE -4, lactate 2.1 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Respiratory distress syndrome from surfactant deficiency
options.rationale: This late preterm infant born by elective caesarean section without antenatal corticosteroids has multiple risk factors for surfactant deficiency. The CXR findings of bilateral ground-glass opacification with air bronchograms and low lung volumes are characteristic of RDS, not TTN. The respiratory acidosis with hypercapnia further supports significant parenchymal lung disease. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Respiratory Distress in the Newborn; RCH CPG: Neonatal Respiratory Distress)
source_refs: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Respiratory Distress in the Newborn

options.text: Transient tachypnoea of the newborn from delayed clearance
options.rationale: TTN classically shows hyperinflated lungs with perihilar streaking, prominent interlobar fissures, and fluid in the horizontal fissure on CXR. This infant has low lung volumes with ground-glass opacity and air bronchograms - features of surfactant deficiency, not retained lung fluid. TTN also typically improves within 24-72 hours with minimal support. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Respiratory Distress in the Newborn)
source_refs: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Respiratory Distress in the Newborn

options.text: Congenital pneumonia from ascending infection at birth
options.rationale: Congenital pneumonia may present with similar CXR findings but typically occurs with risk factors such as prolonged rupture of membranes, maternal fever, or GBS colonisation. This was an elective caesarean section with no infectious risk factors mentioned. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Respiratory Distress)

options.text: Meconium aspiration syndrome following in utero passage
options.rationale: Meconium aspiration syndrome occurs when meconium-stained amniotic fluid is aspirated, producing asymmetric patchy infiltrates and hyperinflation on CXR. It is most common in post-term infants. This preterm infant has no history of meconium-stained liquor and has bilateral symmetrical ground-glass changes. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Respiratory Distress in the Newborn)
source_refs: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Respiratory Distress in the Newborn

options.text: Pneumothorax from barotrauma during initial resuscitation
options.rationale: Pneumothorax on CXR shows unilateral or bilateral lucency with absent lung markings and possibly mediastinal shift. This infant has bilateral ground-glass opacification, which is the opposite finding. No positive-pressure ventilation was described in the resuscitation. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Respiratory Distress)

explanation.summary: Late preterm infants (34-36 weeks) born by elective caesarean section without antenatal corticosteroids are at significant risk of respiratory distress syndrome due to relative surfactant deficiency. The CXR distinction between RDS and TTN is critical: RDS shows low lung volumes with bilateral ground-glass opacity and air bronchograms, while TTN shows hyperinflated lungs with perihilar streaking and fluid in fissures. Late preterm RDS may require surfactant replacement therapy if oxygen requirements escalate despite CPAP. The absence of antenatal corticosteroids in this gestational age range is a modifiable risk factor.
key_points: Late preterm infants born by elective caesarean without antenatal corticosteroids are at high risk of RDS
RDS CXR: low volumes, bilateral ground-glass, air bronchograms; TTN CXR: hyperinflation, perihilar streaking, fluid in fissures
CPAP is the initial respiratory support for RDS, with surfactant given if FiO2 requirements exceed 0.40 on adequate CPAP
Antenatal corticosteroids reduce RDS risk and should be considered for elective caesarean before 38 weeks
Respiratory acidosis with hypercapnia supports significant parenchymal disease rather than simple fluid retention
explanation.pearls: Elective caesarean section at 35-36 weeks without antenatal corticosteroids increases RDS risk 3-4 fold compared with vaginal delivery - RANZCOG recommends considering antenatal corticosteroids for planned caesarean before 38+6 weeks.

sources.label: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Respiratory Distress in the Newborn

subtopic_detail: Duct-dependent cyanotic heart disease - PGE1 initiation
stem: A 6-hour-old term neonate born in a rural hospital develops progressive cyanosis. The infant was well at birth with normal Apgar scores. Oxygen saturations were noted to be 72% on the routine newborn screen at 4 hours of age and have not improved with high-flow oxygen via nasal prongs. The infant is feeding poorly but is not in respiratory distress.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 155, RR 44, SpO2 72% right hand, 70% right foot on 2 L/min O2, Temp 36.6 C
data_table.Examination: Central cyanosis. No respiratory distress. No murmur audible. Femoral pulses palpable. Liver not enlarged. Active and alert.
data_table.Investigations: Hyperoxia test: SpO2 74% and PaO2 35 mmHg after 10 minutes on 100% oxygen via headbox.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intubation with mechanical ventilation at high FiO2
options.rationale: This infant has no respiratory distress and the hypoxaemia is due to cardiac mixing or obstruction, not pulmonary disease. Mechanical ventilation with high FiO2 will not improve oxygenation in duct-dependent cyanotic heart disease and delays the critical intervention of maintaining ductal patency. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic Heart Disease; Queensland Clinical Guideline: Neonatal Stabilisation)

options.text: Urgent echocardiography before commencing any treatment
options.rationale: While echocardiography is essential for definitive diagnosis, waiting for imaging delays treatment in a critically hypoxic neonate. A failed hyperoxia test in a well-perfused neonate without respiratory distress strongly suggests duct-dependent cyanotic heart disease, and prostaglandin should be commenced empirically before echocardiography. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic Heart Disease)

options.text: IV fluid bolus and broad-spectrum antibiotics for sepsis
options.rationale: Neonatal sepsis can cause cyanosis but typically presents with respiratory distress, temperature instability, poor perfusion, and metabolic acidosis. This neonate is well-perfused with no respiratory distress and a failed hyperoxia test, which points to a fixed right-to-left cardiac shunt rather than infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic Heart Disease)

options.text: IV prostaglandin E1 infusion and transfer to cardiac centre
options.rationale: A failed hyperoxia test (PaO2 remains below 100 mmHg on 100% FiO2) in a cyanosed neonate without respiratory distress is diagnostic of cyanotic congenital heart disease. Commencing prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) at 5-10 nanograms/kg/min maintains ductal patency and is life-saving in duct-dependent lesions such as transposition of the great arteries, pulmonary atresia, and critical pulmonary stenosis. The infant should be transferred to a tertiary cardiac centre. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic Heart Disease; Queensland Clinical Guideline: Neonatal Stabilisation)

options.text: Inhaled nitric oxide for persistent pulmonary hypertension
options.rationale: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN) typically presents with labile saturations that improve then deteriorate, and often occurs in the context of meconium aspiration, sepsis, or perinatal asphyxia. A fixed cyanosis unresponsive to 100% oxygen with a failed hyperoxia test favours structural cyanotic heart disease over PPHN. (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic Heart Disease)

explanation.summary: Duct-dependent cyanotic congenital heart disease presents in the first days of life as the ductus arteriosus closes. The hyperoxia test is the critical bedside assessment: failure to raise PaO2 above 100 mmHg (or SpO2 above 85%) on 100% oxygen indicates a fixed right-to-left shunt. Prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) reopens the ductus arteriosus and is life-saving. Common duct-dependent lesions include transposition of the great arteries, pulmonary atresia, tricuspid atresia, and critical pulmonary stenosis. Apnoea is the most common side effect of PGE1, so intubation equipment must be available.
key_points: Failed hyperoxia test (PaO2 below 100 mmHg on 100% O2) indicates cyanotic congenital heart disease
Prostaglandin E1 at 5-10 nanograms/kg/min maintains ductal patency and should be started empirically
Apnoea occurs in 10-12% of neonates on PGE1 - intubation equipment must be immediately available
Pre-ductal and post-ductal saturation difference greater than 3% suggests a ductal-level shunt
Echocardiography confirms the specific lesion but should not delay commencement of PGE1
explanation.pearls: Prostaglandin E1 causes apnoea in 10-12% of neonates - when commencing PGE1 at a rural or regional centre before retrieval, always have intubation equipment at the bedside and consider elective intubation before transport.

subtopic_detail: Protracted bacterial bronchitis - diagnosis and management
stem: A 4-year-old boy presents with a persistent wet productive cough for 6 weeks. There is no wheeze, no night-time cough, and no exercise limitation. He has no personal or family history of atopy or asthma. He is thriving and has had no weight loss. He attends daycare and has had frequent upper respiratory infections this winter but this cough has persisted beyond his last URTI. A trial of salbutamol inhaler made no difference.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 100, RR 22, SpO2 99%, Temp 36.7 C
data_table.Examination: Well-appearing child. No clubbing. Chest: transmitted upper airway sounds. No focal crackles or wheeze. No chest wall deformity.
data_table.Investigations: CXR: clear lung fields, no focal consolidation or lymphadenopathy.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Inhaled corticosteroid trial for 8 weeks for possible asthma
options.rationale: Asthma typically presents with episodic wheeze, dry cough worse at night or with exercise, and response to bronchodilators. This child has a persistent wet cough with no wheeze, no nocturnal symptoms, no exercise limitation, and no bronchodilator response, making asthma unlikely. An ICS trial is not appropriate without asthma features. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Chronic Cough in Children)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Chronic Cough in Children

options.text: Sweat chloride test to exclude cystic fibrosis
options.rationale: Cystic fibrosis should be considered in children with recurrent wet cough, but this child is thriving with a normal CXR and this is his first prolonged episode. Sweat chloride testing becomes appropriate if the cough recurs after adequate antibiotic treatment or if there are other red flags such as failure to thrive, steatorrhoea, or recurrent pneumonia. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Chronic Cough in Children)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Chronic Cough in Children

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for 14 days with reassessment
options.rationale: This presentation is classic for protracted bacterial bronchitis (PBB): a wet productive cough for more than 4 weeks in an otherwise well child with no features of an alternative diagnosis and a normal CXR. First-line treatment per TSANZ guidelines is a 14-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate, targeting the common pathogens (Haemophilus influenzae, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Moraxella catarrhalis). Resolution of cough with antibiotics confirms the diagnosis. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Chronic Cough in Children; eTG: Respiratory)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Chronic Cough in Children

options.text: CT chest and referral to paediatric respiratory physician
options.rationale: CT chest is not indicated as a first-line investigation for chronic wet cough in a well child with a normal CXR. It is reserved for children who fail to respond to appropriate antibiotic therapy or who have red flag features suggesting bronchiectasis or structural abnormality. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Chronic Cough in Children)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Chronic Cough in Children

options.text: Reassurance and observation as this is likely post-viral cough
options.rationale: Post-viral cough is typically dry and resolves within 3-4 weeks. A wet productive cough persisting beyond 4 weeks is not consistent with post-viral aetiology and warrants investigation and treatment. Observation alone risks progression to bronchiectasis. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Chronic Cough in Children)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Chronic Cough in Children

explanation.summary: Protracted bacterial bronchitis is the most common cause of chronic wet cough in preschool children. It is defined by a wet cough lasting more than 4 weeks, absence of features suggesting an alternative diagnosis, and resolution with a 14-day course of appropriate antibiotics. The TSANZ cough guideline recommends amoxicillin-clavulanate as first-line therapy. If the cough recurs more than three times in 12 months, investigation for underlying conditions such as primary ciliary dyskinesia, immunodeficiency, or bronchiectasis is warranted. Untreated PBB may progress to bronchiectasis.
key_points: PBB is defined as wet cough for more than 4 weeks without alternative diagnosis, resolving with antibiotics
Amoxicillin-clavulanate for 14 days is first-line therapy per TSANZ guidelines
Common pathogens are non-typeable Haemophilus influenzae, Streptococcus pneumoniae, and Moraxella catarrhalis
Recurrent PBB (more than 3 episodes per year) warrants investigation for bronchiectasis, immunodeficiency, or PCD
The key distinguishing feature from asthma is a wet (not dry) cough with no wheeze and no bronchodilator response
explanation.pearls: Untreated protracted bacterial bronchitis may progress to bronchiectasis via the 'vicious cycle' hypothesis - chronic bacterial infection leads to neutrophilic airway inflammation, impaired mucociliary clearance, and progressive airway damage within 1-2 years of recurrent episodes.

sources.label: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Chronic Cough in Children

subtopic_detail: Vesicoureteral reflux - management by grade
stem: A 14-month-old girl has had two febrile urinary tract infections in 6 months, both caused by Escherichia coli. After her second episode, a micturating cystourethrogram (MCUG) is performed and shows bilateral vesicoureteral reflux - grade II on the right and grade IV on the left with moderate left ureteric dilatation and blunting of the left renal calyces. A DMSA scan shows a 15% differential function defect in the left kidney with cortical scarring at the upper pole.

data_table.Investigations: MCUG: right VUR grade II, left VUR grade IV. DMSA scan: left kidney 35% differential function (normal > 45%), upper pole cortical scar. Right kidney 65% differential function, normal uptake.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Observation with prompt treatment of future symptomatic UTIs
options.rationale: Observation alone may be appropriate for low-grade VUR (grades I-II) without renal scarring. This child has grade IV reflux with documented renal scarring and recurrent febrile UTIs, warranting a more proactive approach to prevent further renal damage. (Source: RCH CPG: Vesicoureteral Reflux; KHA-CARI Guidelines)

options.text: High-dose intermittent antibiotic therapy for acute episodes only
options.rationale: Treating only acute episodes without prophylaxis exposes the scarred kidney to further damage with each febrile UTI. In a child with high-grade reflux and existing renal scarring, the risk of progressive renal damage from recurrent pyelonephritis is significant. (Source: KHA-CARI Guidelines)

options.text: Bilateral ureteric reimplantation surgery within 4 weeks
options.rationale: Surgical reimplantation is effective for high-grade VUR but is not the immediate first step. A trial of antibiotic prophylaxis is generally recommended first, with surgery reserved for breakthrough febrile UTIs on prophylaxis, worsening renal scarring, or non-resolution of high-grade reflux. The grade II right side is also unlikely to require surgery. (Source: RCH CPG: Vesicoureteral Reflux)

options.text: Endoscopic subureteric injection of bulking agent bilaterally
options.rationale: Endoscopic injection (e.g. Deflux) is an option for VUR but has lower success rates for grade IV-V reflux compared with open reimplantation. It is not the recommended first-line approach before trialling antibiotic prophylaxis, especially for high-grade reflux with renal scarring. (Source: RCH CPG: Vesicoureteral Reflux)

options.text: Continuous low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis with regular follow-up
options.rationale: For a young child with recurrent febrile UTIs, grade IV VUR, and existing renal scarring, continuous antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g. trimethoprim) is recommended to reduce UTI recurrence while allowing time for spontaneous resolution. Regular follow-up includes repeat MCUG at 12-18 months and repeat DMSA if breakthrough UTIs occur. If breakthrough febrile UTIs occur on prophylaxis, surgical options should be discussed. (Source: RCH CPG: Vesicoureteral Reflux; KHA-CARI Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Vesicoureteral reflux management depends on grade, presence of renal scarring, and frequency of breakthrough UTIs. Low-grade VUR (I-III) without scarring often resolves spontaneously and may be managed conservatively. High-grade VUR (IV-V) with renal scarring carries a higher risk of progressive renal damage and warrants continuous antibiotic prophylaxis. Surgical intervention (open reimplantation or endoscopic injection) is reserved for cases failing medical management. Serial imaging monitors for reflux resolution and new scarring.
key_points: VUR is graded I-V based on the degree of ureteric dilatation and calyceal blunting on MCUG
Antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended for high-grade VUR with renal scarring to prevent further damage
Spontaneous resolution rates decrease with increasing VUR grade: approximately 80% for grade I-II vs 30% for grade IV
DMSA scan is the gold standard for detecting renal cortical scarring
Surgical reimplantation is reserved for breakthrough febrile UTIs on prophylaxis or non-resolving high-grade reflux
explanation.pearls: Trimethoprim at one-third of the therapeutic dose given as a single nightly dose is the standard prophylactic regimen for VUR in Australian practice - nitrofurantoin is an alternative but is avoided in infants under 1 month due to haemolytic risk.

subtopic_detail: JIA oligoarticular - uveitis screening
stem: A 3-year-old girl is diagnosed with oligoarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis affecting her right knee and left ankle. She is ANA-positive at a titre of 1:640. She has no eye complaints, no redness of the eyes, and no photophobia. Her parents ask whether any additional screening is required.

data_table.Examination: Right knee: mild effusion, limited extension by 10 degrees. Left ankle: mild swelling, full range of motion. No enthesitis. No psoriatic rash. Eyes: no conjunctival injection on external examination.
data_table.Investigations: ANA positive 1:640. RF negative. HLA-B27 negative. ESR 28 mm/hr, CRP 12 mg/L.

lead_in: What is the most important additional screening for this child?

options.text: Slit-lamp ophthalmology review every 3 months for uveitis
options.rationale: This child has the highest-risk profile for JIA-associated uveitis: young age at onset (under 7 years), oligoarticular JIA, and ANA-positive. JIA-associated anterior uveitis is typically chronic and asymptomatic (no pain or redness), meaning it can only be detected by slit-lamp examination. Without screening, uveitis can progress to posterior synechiae, cataract, band keratopathy, and blindness. Australian and international guidelines recommend 3-monthly slit-lamp review for high-risk patients. (Source: RACS/RACP Guidelines: JIA Uveitis Screening; Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology)

options.text: Annual cardiac echocardiography for valvular involvement
options.rationale: Cardiac involvement is a feature of acute rheumatic fever, not JIA. JIA does not cause valvular disease. Pericarditis can occur in systemic-onset JIA but not oligoarticular JIA, and routine echocardiography is not indicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology)

options.text: Six-monthly renal function and urinalysis for nephritis
options.rationale: Renal involvement is not a feature of JIA. Nephritis is associated with systemic lupus erythematosus, IgA vasculitis, and other conditions but not with oligoarticular JIA. ANA positivity in JIA predicts uveitis risk, not renal disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology)

options.text: Baseline and 6-monthly DEXA scan for osteoporosis screening
options.rationale: Osteoporosis screening may be considered in children on prolonged systemic corticosteroids or with systemic JIA, but is not routine for newly diagnosed oligoarticular JIA. This child's disease can likely be managed with intra-articular corticosteroids and does not require systemic therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology)

options.text: Six-monthly liver function tests for methotrexate toxicity
options.rationale: LFT monitoring is required for children on methotrexate, but this child has just been diagnosed and methotrexate has not been started. Oligoarticular JIA is often managed with intra-articular corticosteroid injections as first-line, with methotrexate reserved for refractory or polyarticular disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology)

explanation.summary: JIA-associated anterior uveitis is the most common extra-articular complication of oligoarticular JIA. It is characteristically chronic, bilateral, and asymptomatic (white eye) - children do not complain of pain or redness. Risk factors for uveitis include young age at JIA onset (under 7 years), ANA positivity, oligoarticular subtype, and female sex. Without regular slit-lamp screening, up to 30% of high-risk children develop uveitis-related complications including posterior synechiae, cataracts, and glaucoma. Screening frequency is determined by risk stratification.
key_points: JIA-associated uveitis is chronic and asymptomatic - slit-lamp examination is the only way to detect it early
Highest risk: ANA-positive, oligoarticular JIA, onset under 7 years, female
Screening frequency for high-risk patients is every 3 months for the first 4 years after JIA diagnosis
Complications of undetected uveitis include posterior synechiae, band keratopathy, cataracts, glaucoma, and blindness
Uveitis can precede, coincide with, or follow the onset of arthritis
explanation.pearls: JIA-associated uveitis is a 'white eye' uveitis - the absence of conjunctival redness, pain, or photophobia means it is invisible to parents and GPs, and slit-lamp screening is the only reliable detection method for the first 7 years after JIA diagnosis.

subtopic_detail: Langerhans cell histiocytosis - multisystem presentation
stem: A 2-year-old boy presents with a 6-week history of a scaly erythematous rash on his scalp that has not responded to antifungal treatment. His parents also report increased thirst and frequent wet nappies over the past 3 weeks. On examination there is a palpable firm non-tender swelling over the right parietal bone. He has mild hepatomegaly and a seborrhoea-like rash involving the scalp, postauricular area, and groin.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 120, Temp 37.0 C, weight 12 kg (25th centile)
data_table.Examination: Scaly erythematous plaques on scalp, behind ears, and in inguinal folds. Firm 2 cm non-tender swelling right parietal region. Liver palpable 3 cm below costal margin. Spleen not palpable.
data_table.Investigations: FBC: Hb 98 g/L, WCC 11.2 x10^9/L, platelets 145 x10^9/L. Urine specific gravity 1.002 (dilute). Serum sodium 147 mmol/L. Skull X-ray: well-defined lytic lesion right parietal bone with no sclerotic margin.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?

options.text: Skin scraping for fungal microscopy and culture
options.rationale: The rash has already failed antifungal therapy and the combination of a seborrhoea-like rash with a lytic skull lesion, hepatomegaly, and diabetes insipidus is strongly suggestive of a systemic infiltrative process rather than a dermatophyte infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis)

options.text: Biopsy of the skull lesion with CD1a immunohistochemistry
options.rationale: The combination of a seborrhoea-like rash refractory to antifungals, a punched-out lytic skull lesion, hepatomegaly, anaemia, and diabetes insipidus (polyuria with dilute urine and hypernatraemia) is the classic multisystem presentation of Langerhans cell histiocytosis. Tissue biopsy with positive CD1a and CD207 (langerin) immunohistochemistry on the infiltrating cells confirms the diagnosis. Birbeck granules on electron microscopy are the historical gold standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis; Histiocyte Society Guidelines)

options.text: Whole-body MRI for metastatic neuroblastoma staging
options.rationale: Neuroblastoma can cause lytic bone lesions but typically presents with a primary abdominal or thoracic mass, proptosis from orbital metastases, or raccoon eyes. The combination of seborrhoeic rash, punched-out lytic lesion, and diabetes insipidus is more characteristic of LCH. MIBG scan is the staging investigation for neuroblastoma. (Source: RCH CPG: Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis)

options.text: Bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy for leukaemia
options.rationale: Acute leukaemia can cause bone pain, hepatomegaly, and cytopenias, but does not typically cause well-defined lytic skull lesions or a seborrhoeic rash. While bone marrow assessment may be part of the LCH staging workup, the diagnostic biopsy should target the most accessible lesion - in this case the skull or skin. (Source: Histiocyte Society Guidelines)

options.text: Water deprivation test to confirm diabetes insipidus
options.rationale: The clinical picture of polyuria with dilute urine (specific gravity 1.002) and hypernatraemia is consistent with diabetes insipidus, but confirming DI alone does not identify the underlying cause. The priority is to diagnose the systemic disease causing the DI - tissue biopsy is the key investigation. (Source: RCH CPG: Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis)

explanation.summary: Langerhans cell histiocytosis is a clonal proliferation of pathological Langerhans-type dendritic cells that can affect virtually any organ. The classic triad of skull lytic lesions, diabetes insipidus, and exophthalmos (Hand-Schuller-Christian disease) is rare, but multisystem LCH in young children commonly involves skin, bone, liver, and the pituitary stalk. The seborrhoeic dermatitis-like rash of LCH is often misdiagnosed as cradle cap or fungal infection. Diabetes insipidus from pituitary stalk infiltration occurs in 15-25% of multisystem cases. Diagnosis requires tissue biopsy demonstrating CD1a-positive and CD207-positive histiocytes.
key_points: LCH skin involvement mimics seborrhoeic dermatitis but fails to respond to standard treatment
Well-defined 'punched-out' lytic bone lesions without sclerotic margins are characteristic on plain X-ray
Diabetes insipidus indicates pituitary stalk infiltration and occurs in 15-25% of multisystem LCH
Diagnosis requires CD1a and CD207 (langerin) positivity on immunohistochemistry of biopsied tissue
Risk organ involvement (liver, spleen, bone marrow) determines prognosis and treatment intensity
explanation.pearls: In children under 2 years, LCH involving risk organs (liver, spleen, or bone marrow) carries a mortality of 10-20% despite chemotherapy - early identification of hepatomegaly and cytopenias is critical for risk stratification.

subtopic_detail: IgA vasculitis - renal monitoring and biopsy threshold
stem: A 6-year-old boy was diagnosed with IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schonlein purpura) 3 weeks ago with a typical purpuric rash over the lower limbs and buttocks, arthralgia, and colicky abdominal pain. His acute symptoms have resolved with supportive care. At routine follow-up today, his urine dipstick shows 2+ protein and 2+ blood. His blood pressure is 108/72 mmHg (above the 95th centile for age and height).

data_table.Vital signs: HR 90, BP 108/72 mmHg (>95th centile), Temp 36.6 C
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: protein 2+, blood 2+, no casts on microscopy. Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio (uPCR): 120 mg/mmol (normal < 20 mg/mmol). Serum creatinine 48 micromol/L (normal for age). Serum albumin 30 g/L (mildly low). Complement C3 and C4: normal.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassurance and repeat urinalysis in 4 weeks as routine
options.rationale: While mild microscopic haematuria alone may be observed in IgA vasculitis, this child has significant proteinuria (uPCR 120 mg/mmol), hypertension, and hypoalbuminaemia - indicating nephritis with nephrotic-range features. This requires active management rather than simple observation. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura; KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guidelines)

options.text: Oral prednisolone and ACE inhibitor with nephrology review
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are appropriate for reducing proteinuria and controlling hypertension, and may be started. However, the degree of proteinuria and hypoalbuminaemia in this child suggests significant glomerular involvement that may require biopsy before deciding on immunosuppressive therapy. Starting prednisolone without histological confirmation of the severity risks under-treating or over-treating. (Source: KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guidelines)

options.text: Immunoglobulin A level and ANCA panel for reclassification
options.rationale: Serum IgA may be elevated in IgA vasculitis but is not specific and does not change management. ANCA testing is relevant for ANCA-associated vasculitis (GPA, MPA) but IgA vasculitis is not ANCA-associated. The diagnosis is clinical and additional serological testing does not inform the management of nephritis. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Renal biopsy and paediatric nephrology referral
options.rationale: This child has IgA vasculitis nephritis with nephrotic-range proteinuria (uPCR > 100 mg/mmol), hypertension, and hypoalbuminaemia. These features indicate moderate-to-severe glomerular involvement and meet the threshold for renal biopsy.
