Biopsy determines the ISKDC histological grade (I-VI), which guides immunosuppressive therapy. Nephrology referral is essential for biopsy and ongoing management. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura; KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guidelines)

options.text: IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy for 3 days
options.rationale: IV methylprednisolone may be used for severe crescentic IgA vasculitis nephritis, but should not be commenced without first obtaining a renal biopsy to confirm the severity and guide treatment. Starting aggressive immunosuppression empirically risks unnecessary toxicity if the histological grade does not warrant it. (Source: KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guidelines)

explanation.summary: IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schonlein purpura) nephritis occurs in up to 50% of affected children, usually within the first 3 months. Most have mild disease with isolated haematuria that resolves spontaneously. However, significant proteinuria (uPCR > 100 mg/mmol), nephrotic syndrome, hypertension, or impaired renal function indicate moderate-to-severe nephritis requiring renal biopsy. The ISKDC classification grades severity from I (minimal change) to VI (membranoproliferative) and guides immunosuppressive therapy. Long-term renal outcomes are excellent for mild disease but 5-10% of those with nephritis develop chronic kidney disease.
key_points: Nephritis occurs in up to 50% of IgA vasculitis cases, usually within the first 3 months
Renal biopsy is indicated for nephrotic-range proteinuria (uPCR > 100 mg/mmol), nephrotic syndrome, or impaired renal function
Urinalysis should be monitored weekly for 4 weeks then monthly for 6 months after IgA vasculitis diagnosis
ISKDC classification on biopsy guides treatment - grades III-V may require immunosuppression
Normal complement levels help distinguish IgA vasculitis nephritis from post-infectious glomerulonephritis and lupus nephritis
explanation.pearls: Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio above 100 mg/mmol in IgA vasculitis nephritis is the commonly used threshold for renal biopsy - this corresponds to approximately 1 g/day proteinuria and predicts ISKDC grade III or higher on histology.

subtopic_detail: Acute pancreatitis in child - initial management
stem: A 10-year-old girl presents with acute onset epigastric pain radiating to the back, vomiting, and anorexia for 12 hours. She has no prior abdominal complaints. She takes sodium valproate for epilepsy. On examination she lies still and resists any movement. Her abdomen is tender in the epigastrium with guarding but no rebound.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 120, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 24, Temp 37.8 C
data_table.Examination: Epigastric tenderness with voluntary guarding. No distension. Bowel sounds reduced. No jaundice.
data_table.Investigations: Serum lipase 1450 U/L (normal < 60 U/L). Serum amylase 680 U/L (normal < 100 U/L). ALT 45 U/L. Bilirubin 15 micromol/L. Calcium 2.20 mmol/L. Triglycerides 1.8 mmol/L. Abdominal ultrasound: diffusely swollen pancreas with peripancreatic fluid. No gallstones. Common bile duct not dilated.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Urgent ERCP for suspected common bile duct obstruction
options.rationale: ERCP is indicated for biliary pancreatitis with choledocholithiasis and cholangitis. This child has no gallstones on ultrasound, no bile duct dilatation, and normal bilirubin and ALT, making biliary obstruction unlikely. The likely aetiology is drug-induced (valproate). (Source: NASPGHAN: Paediatric Pancreatitis)

options.text: Commence total parenteral nutrition with bowel rest
options.rationale: Prolonged bowel rest and TPN are no longer recommended for acute pancreatitis. Early enteral feeding (within 24-48 hours) is associated with shorter hospital stays, fewer complications, and reduced infectious morbidity. TPN is reserved for patients who cannot tolerate enteral feeding after 5-7 days. (Source: NASPGHAN: Paediatric Pancreatitis; eTG: Gastrointestinal)

options.text: Prophylactic IV antibiotics with imipenem for prevention
options.rationale: Prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended for acute pancreatitis as they do not reduce the risk of infected necrosis and promote antimicrobial resistance. Antibiotics are only indicated if there is documented infected necrosis or concurrent cholangitis. (Source: NASPGHAN: Paediatric Pancreatitis)

options.text: IV fluid resuscitation, analgesia, and early enteral feeding
options.rationale: The management of acute pancreatitis in children centres on aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (1.5-2 times maintenance crystalloid), effective analgesia (paracetamol, opioids if needed), and early enteral nutrition within 24-48 hours as tolerated. Valproate should be reviewed as a likely causative agent. Monitoring for complications (necrosis, pseudocyst, organ failure) with serial lipase and clinical assessment is essential. (Source: NASPGHAN: Paediatric Pancreatitis; RCH CPG: Pancreatitis)

options.text: Immediate surgical necrosectomy for pancreatic necrosis
options.rationale: Surgical necrosectomy is reserved for infected pancreatic necrosis confirmed by fine-needle aspiration or clinical deterioration, and is typically delayed until at least 4 weeks to allow walled-off necrosis to mature. Imaging shows peripancreatic fluid but not established necrosis, and surgery at this stage is premature. (Source: NASPGHAN: Paediatric Pancreatitis)

explanation.summary: Acute pancreatitis in children is increasingly recognised, with drug-induced causes (valproate, azathioprine, L-asparaginase) being an important aetiology alongside biliary, traumatic, and idiopathic causes. Modern management has shifted away from prolonged bowel rest toward early enteral feeding within 24-48 hours. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation reduces the risk of pancreatic necrosis. Valproate-induced pancreatitis typically occurs within the first year of treatment and requires drug cessation and alternative anticonvulsant selection. Severity is assessed by clinical parameters and imaging.
key_points: Serum lipase is more sensitive and specific than amylase for diagnosing acute pancreatitis
Early enteral nutrition within 24-48 hours is superior to prolonged bowel rest and TPN
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (1.5-2 times maintenance) reduces the risk of necrosis
Valproate is a recognised cause of pancreatitis in children and should be discontinued
Prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated in acute pancreatitis
explanation.pearls: Valproate-induced pancreatitis can occur at any time during treatment but most cases present within the first year - the drug must be permanently discontinued and never re-challenged, as recurrence risk exceeds 50% on re-exposure.

subtopic_detail: OSA in child - adenotonsillectomy referral criteria
stem: A 5-year-old boy is referred by his GP for assessment of snoring. His parents report loud habitual snoring every night for the past 12 months, witnessed apnoeic pauses, restless sleep, and mouth breathing. He has daytime behavioural difficulties including inattention at school. His BMI is at the 75th centile. He has grade 3+ tonsillar hypertrophy bilaterally and chronic mouth breathing with adenoid facies.

data_table.Examination: Grade 3+ tonsillar hypertrophy bilaterally (tonsils meeting in the midline). High arched palate. Mouth breathing at rest. No pectus excavatum. BMI 75th centile.
data_table.Investigations: Overnight polysomnography: obstructive apnoea-hypopnoea index (oAHI) 12 events/hour (normal < 1), SpO2 nadir 84%, peak end-tidal CO2 55 mmHg.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Intranasal mometasone spray for 12 weeks with review
options.rationale: Intranasal corticosteroids may be trialled for mild OSA (oAHI 1-5) or as an adjunct, but this child has moderate-to-severe OSA with an oAHI of 12 and significant desaturations. Medical therapy alone is inadequate for this severity. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association: Paediatric OSA; RCH CPG: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea)

options.text: CPAP therapy titration and long-term home CPAP use
options.rationale: CPAP is second-line for paediatric OSA and is reserved for children who are not surgical candidates, who have persistent OSA after adenotonsillectomy, or who have conditions where surgery is unlikely to be curative (e.g. obesity, craniofacial abnormalities). In a child with adenotonsillar hypertrophy as the primary cause, surgery is first-line. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association: Paediatric OSA)

options.text: Weight management program and repeat polysomnography in 1 year
options.rationale: This child's BMI is at the 75th centile, which is normal weight. While obesity worsens OSA, it is not the primary driver here - the grade 3+ tonsillar hypertrophy is the clear anatomical cause. Delaying treatment for a year risks neurocognitive and cardiovascular consequences of untreated moderate-to-severe OSA. (Source: RCH CPG: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea)

options.text: Mandibular advancement device fitted by dental specialist
options.rationale: Mandibular advancement devices are used in adult OSA but are not recommended in children due to the growing craniofacial skeleton. Adenotonsillectomy is the first-line surgical treatment for paediatric OSA caused by lymphoid hypertrophy. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association: Paediatric OSA)

options.text: Referral for adenotonsillectomy with post-operative monitoring
options.rationale: Adenotonsillectomy is the first-line treatment for moderate-to-severe paediatric OSA due to adenotonsillar hypertrophy. This child has an oAHI of 12 (moderate-severe), significant desaturations, and grade 3+ tonsils. The CHAT trial demonstrated significant improvements in behaviour, quality of life, and polysomnographic parameters after surgery. Post-operative monitoring in hospital is recommended given the severity of his OSA due to increased risk of post-operative respiratory complications. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association: Paediatric OSA; RCH CPG: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea)

explanation.summary: Paediatric obstructive sleep apnoea affects 1-5% of children and is most commonly caused by adenotonsillar hypertrophy in the 2-8 year age group. Polysomnography is the gold standard for diagnosis and severity assessment. An oAHI greater than 1 event per hour is abnormal in children. Adenotonsillectomy is first-line treatment for moderate-to-severe OSA with adenotonsillar hypertrophy, with cure rates of 70-80%. Children with severe OSA require post-operative monitoring due to increased risk of respiratory complications. Persistent OSA after surgery warrants CPAP therapy.
key_points: Adenotonsillectomy is first-line treatment for paediatric OSA caused by adenotonsillar hypertrophy
An obstructive AHI greater than 1 per hour is abnormal in children (unlike adults where the threshold is 5)
Post-operative monitoring is recommended for oAHI greater than 10, SpO2 nadir below 85%, or age under 3 years
Intranasal corticosteroids may be trialled for mild OSA (oAHI 1-5) but are insufficient for moderate-to-severe disease
Residual OSA after adenotonsillectomy occurs in 20-30% of cases and may require CPAP
explanation.pearls: The paediatric oAHI threshold for abnormality is 1 event per hour - far lower than the adult threshold of 5 - reflecting the developing brain's greater vulnerability to intermittent hypoxia and sleep fragmentation.

subtopic_detail: Stridor in infant - laryngomalacia assessment
stem: A 6-week-old infant presents with inspiratory stridor that has been present since 2 weeks of age. The stridor is worse during feeding and when the infant is supine. It improves when the infant is prone or calm. She is exclusively breastfed and feeding well, gaining weight along the 50th centile. There is no history of intubation or respiratory distress at birth.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 140, RR 38, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 36.8 C, weight 4.2 kg (50th centile)
data_table.Examination: Intermittent inspiratory stridor at rest, worsening with agitation and feeding. No biphasic stridor. No subcostal recession at rest. No cyanosis. Good air entry bilaterally.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial investigation?

options.text: Flexible nasendoscopy in the outpatient ENT clinic
options.rationale: Flexible nasendoscopy (fibre-optic laryngoscopy) performed awake in clinic is the gold standard for diagnosing laryngomalacia. It directly visualises the supraglottic structures and demonstrates the characteristic omega-shaped epiglottis with prolapse of the aryepiglottic folds during inspiration. It can be performed at the bedside without sedation and also excludes other causes of stridor such as vocal cord palsy or subglottic lesions. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor in Children; Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand

options.text: Lateral neck X-ray to assess the subglottic airway
options.rationale: A lateral neck X-ray may show the 'steeple sign' in croup or a retropharyngeal mass, but has poor sensitivity for laryngomalacia. The supraglottic structures are not well visualised on plain film. Direct visualisation with flexible endoscopy is far superior. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor in Children)

options.text: CT neck with contrast for structural airway assessment
options.rationale: CT neck exposes the infant to ionising radiation and requires sedation, which can worsen airway obstruction. It is reserved for suspected vascular rings, deep neck infections, or complex airway abnormalities not diagnosable by endoscopy. Laryngomalacia is best diagnosed by direct visualisation. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor in Children)

options.text: Microlaryngoscopy and bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Rigid microlaryngoscopy and bronchoscopy (MLB) under general anaesthesia provides the most comprehensive airway assessment but is reserved for severe cases requiring intervention (supraglottoplasty), diagnostic uncertainty after flexible endoscopy, or suspected synchronous airway lesions. An awake flexible scope is the appropriate first investigation. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor in Children)

options.text: Barium swallow to assess for vascular ring compression
options.rationale: A barium swallow can identify extrinsic oesophageal compression from a vascular ring, which typically causes biphasic (inspiratory and expiratory) stridor and feeding difficulties. This infant has purely inspiratory stridor that worsens in the supine position - a pattern more consistent with supraglottic collapse (laryngomalacia) than vascular compression. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor in Children)

explanation.summary: Laryngomalacia is the most common cause of stridor in infants, accounting for 60-70% of congenital stridor. It is caused by collapse of the supraglottic structures during inspiration due to immature cartilaginous support. Symptoms typically onset in the first 2-4 weeks of life, peak at 6-8 months, and resolve by 12-24 months. Most cases are mild and self-limiting. Severe laryngomalacia (failure to thrive, cyanotic episodes, obstructive sleep apnoea) occurs in 10-15% of cases and may require supraglottoplasty. Flexible nasendoscopy is the diagnostic investigation of choice.
key_points: Laryngomalacia accounts for 60-70% of congenital stridor in infants
Classic features: inspiratory stridor worsening in supine position and during feeding, improving when prone
Flexible nasendoscopy performed awake is the gold standard diagnostic investigation
Most cases are mild and self-limiting, resolving by 12-24 months
Supraglottoplasty is indicated for severe cases with failure to thrive, cyanosis, or cor pulmonale
explanation.pearls: Approximately 15-20% of infants with laryngomalacia have a synchronous second airway lesion (subglottic stenosis, tracheomalacia, or vocal cord palsy) - if symptoms are disproportionately severe for the degree of laryngomalacia seen on flexible scope, a full airway assessment under general anaesthesia is warranted.

sources.label: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand

subtopic_detail: Childhood obesity - metabolic screening
stem: A 10-year-old boy is referred by his GP for weight management. His BMI is 28.5 kg/m2, placing him above the 99th centile for age. He has acanthosis nigricans on the posterior neck and axillae. His mother has type 2 diabetes. He has no symptoms of obstructive sleep apnoea and is normotensive. He is Tanner stage 2 for pubic hair.

data_table.Vital signs: Height 143 cm (75th centile), weight 58.3 kg (>99th centile), BMI 28.5 kg/m2 (>99th centile), BP 112/68 mmHg (normal)
data_table.Examination: Acanthosis nigricans posterior neck and axillae bilaterally. Abdominal obesity. No striae. Normal external genitalia. Tanner stage 2.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial metabolic screening for this child?

options.text: Oral glucose tolerance test and serum insulin level
options.rationale: While an OGTT can diagnose impaired glucose tolerance and type 2 diabetes, fasting glucose and HbA1c are the recommended initial screening tests in Australian guidelines for paediatric obesity. An OGTT is reserved for equivocal results or high-risk populations. Fasting insulin is not routinely recommended as there is no standardised paediatric reference range. (Source: NHMRC: Clinical Practice Guidelines for Obesity; RCH CPG: Obesity)
source_refs: NHMRC: Clinical Practice Guidelines for Obesity

options.text: Fasting lipids, glucose, HbA1c, and liver function tests
options.rationale: Australian guidelines recommend a comprehensive metabolic screen for children with obesity (BMI above the 95th centile) including fasting lipid profile (total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, triglycerides), fasting glucose, HbA1c, and liver function tests (screening for non-alcoholic fatty liver disease). Acanthosis nigricans and a family history of type 2 diabetes increase the risk of insulin resistance and dyslipidaemia. (Source: NHMRC: Clinical Practice Guidelines for Obesity; RCH CPG: Obesity; Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)
source_refs: NHMRC: Clinical Practice Guidelines for Obesity

options.text: Thyroid function tests and early morning cortisol only
options.rationale: Hypothyroidism and Cushing syndrome are rare causes of obesity in children. Thyroid function may be checked but is not the most appropriate initial screen on its own. Hypothyroidism causing obesity is almost always associated with short stature - this child is at the 75th centile for height, making endocrine causes unlikely. (Source: RCH CPG: Obesity)

options.text: Leptin level and genetic testing for monogenic obesity
options.rationale: Monogenic obesity (leptin deficiency, MC4R mutations) is extremely rare and typically presents with severe early-onset obesity before age 2 years, extreme hyperphagia, and sometimes associated features. This 10-year-old's presentation is consistent with common multifactorial obesity. Genetic testing is not indicated as an initial screen. (Source: NHMRC: Clinical Practice Guidelines for Obesity)
source_refs: NHMRC: Clinical Practice Guidelines for Obesity

options.text: Overnight polysomnography and echocardiography screening
options.rationale: Polysomnography is indicated if there are symptoms of obstructive sleep apnoea, but this child has no OSA symptoms. Echocardiography is not part of routine metabolic screening for childhood obesity unless there are cardiac symptoms or signs. These investigations may be considered but are not the appropriate initial metabolic screen. (Source: RCH CPG: Obesity)

explanation.summary: Childhood obesity above the 95th centile BMI warrants metabolic screening for comorbidities. The NHMRC guidelines recommend fasting lipids, fasting glucose, HbA1c, and liver function tests as the core metabolic panel. Acanthosis nigricans is a cutaneous marker of insulin resistance and should prompt glucose metabolism assessment. Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease affects up to 40% of obese children and is detected by elevated ALT. Lifestyle modification (healthy eating, physical activity, reduced screen time) is the cornerstone of management, with pharmacotherapy (metformin, liraglutide) reserved for specific circumstances.
key_points: BMI above the 95th centile in children warrants metabolic screening with fasting lipids, glucose, HbA1c, and LFTs
Acanthosis nigricans indicates insulin resistance and increases the risk of type 2 diabetes
Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (ALT elevation) affects up to 40% of obese children
Normal height velocity makes endocrine causes of obesity (hypothyroidism, Cushing) very unlikely
Family history of type 2 diabetes is a major risk factor for childhood insulin resistance
explanation.pearls: In an obese child, a normal or tall height centile with crossing weight centiles indicates exogenous (multifactorial) obesity - a short or falling height centile with rising weight should prompt investigation for endocrine causes including Cushing syndrome and hypothyroidism.

sources.label: NHMRC: Clinical Practice Guidelines for Obesity

subtopic_detail: Congenital syphilis - investigation and treatment
stem: A term neonate is born to a mother who was found to have reactive syphilis serology (RPR titre 1:64, TPPA positive) at 32 weeks gestation. She received a single dose of benzathine penicillin at 33 weeks but no further treatment or follow-up serology. The neonate appears well at birth but the paediatric team is called to assess for possible congenital syphilis.

data_table.Examination: Well-appearing term neonate. No rash. No hepatosplenomegaly. No rhinitis. Fontanelles soft and flat.
data_table.Investigations: Neonatal serology: RPR titre 1:32. FBC: Hb 165 g/L, WCC 14 x10^9/L, platelets 180 x10^9/L. LFTs: ALT 28 U/L, ALP 180 U/L (normal neonatal). Long bone X-rays: no periostitis or metaphyseal changes.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of this neonate?

options.text: Observation only as maternal treatment was adequate
options.rationale: Maternal treatment with a single dose of benzathine penicillin at 33 weeks is inadequate. Adequate treatment requires benzathine penicillin administered at least 30 days before delivery, with documented serological response (four-fold RPR titre decline). A single dose given 7 weeks before delivery without follow-up serology does not meet criteria for adequate treatment. (Source: ASHM: Australian STI Management Guidelines; SA Health: Congenital Syphilis)

options.text: Repeat neonatal serology in 3 months for titre comparison
options.rationale: While serial serology is required for monitoring, delaying treatment to observe titre trends is inappropriate when maternal treatment is inadequate. Passively transferred maternal antibodies may decline regardless of neonatal infection status, and untreated congenital syphilis can cause irreversible damage. (Source: ASHM: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin for 10 days with follow-up serology
options.rationale: When maternal treatment is inadequate (administered less than 30 days before delivery, incomplete course, or no documented serological response), the neonate should be treated presumptively for congenital syphilis regardless of clinical appearance. The standard regimen is IV benzylpenicillin (50 mg/kg/dose, 12-hourly in the first week then 8-hourly for the remaining days) for 10 days. Follow-up serology at 3, 6, and 12 months confirms treatment response. (Source: ASHM: Australian STI Management Guidelines; SA Health: Congenital Syphilis)

options.text: Single dose IM benzathine penicillin to the neonate
options.rationale: A single dose of IM benzathine penicillin may be appropriate only if the neonate is fully investigated and all results are normal AND the mother was adequately treated. When maternal treatment is inadequate, a single dose is insufficient and the full 10-day IV regimen is required. (Source: ASHM: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

options.text: Lumbar puncture only and treat based on CSF findings
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture to assess for neurosyphilis (CSF VDRL, cell count, protein) is part of the workup for congenital syphilis and should be performed before starting treatment. However, treatment should not be withheld pending CSF results, and a normal CSF does not change the need for a 10-day IV course when maternal treatment is inadequate. (Source: SA Health: Congenital Syphilis)

explanation.summary: Congenital syphilis rates have risen sharply in Australia, particularly in northern and central Australia. Adequate maternal treatment requires benzathine penicillin administered at least 30 days before delivery with a documented four-fold decline in RPR titre. When these criteria are not met, the neonate must be treated presumptively with 10 days of IV benzylpenicillin regardless of clinical appearance, as early congenital syphilis may be asymptomatic at birth. The workup includes FBC, LFTs, long bone X-rays, ophthalmology review, audiology, and lumbar puncture.
key_points: Adequate maternal syphilis treatment requires benzathine penicillin at least 30 days before delivery with documented serological response
IV benzylpenicillin for 10 days is the standard treatment for presumed congenital syphilis
Up to 60% of neonates with congenital syphilis are asymptomatic at birth - normal examination does not exclude infection
Follow-up serology at 3, 6, and 12 months is required to confirm treatment response
Congenital syphilis rates have increased significantly in Australia since 2015, particularly in remote Aboriginal communities
explanation.pearls: A neonatal RPR titre equal to or less than the maternal titre may represent passive transfer of antibodies, but a titre four-fold or greater above the maternal titre is diagnostic of congenital infection - however, treatment should never be delayed for titre comparison when maternal treatment is inadequate.

subtopic_detail: Obstetric cholestasis - bile acid threshold and delivery timing
stem: A 34-year-old woman at 35 weeks gestation presents with a 2-week history of intense generalised pruritus that is worse at night, concentrated on the palms and soles. She has no rash apart from excoriations. Her pregnancy has been otherwise uncomplicated. She has no history of liver disease. Her sister had a similar presentation in her third trimester.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 82, Temp 36.7 C
data_table.Investigations: Serum bile acids: 55 micromol/L (normal < 10 micromol/L). ALT 85 U/L (mildly elevated). Bilirubin 18 micromol/L (normal). Hepatitis B and C serology: negative. Liver ultrasound: normal.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Ursodeoxycholic acid and delivery at 37 weeks gestation
options.rationale: While ursodeoxycholic acid is first-line symptomatic therapy, bile acids above 40 micromol/L classify this as severe obstetric cholestasis. RANZCOG guidelines recommend delivery at 36 weeks (not 37 weeks) for bile acids above 40 micromol/L due to the increased risk of stillbirth. (Source: RANZCOG: Obstetric Cholestasis; RCOG Green-top Guideline: Obstetric Cholestasis)

options.text: Topical emollients and antihistamines until spontaneous labour
options.rationale: Expectant management until spontaneous labour is inappropriate for obstetric cholestasis. Bile acids above 10 micromol/L in pregnancy are associated with adverse foetal outcomes including meconium-stained liquor, preterm birth, and stillbirth. The risk of stillbirth increases significantly above 40 micromol/L. Active management with delivery timing is required. (Source: RANZCOG: Obstetric Cholestasis)

options.text: Cholestyramine and weekly bile acid monitoring until term
options.rationale: Cholestyramine is poorly tolerated and less effective than ursodeoxycholic acid for obstetric cholestasis pruritus. Weekly bile acid monitoring alone without planned delivery timing does not address the stillbirth risk. Bile acids above 40 micromol/L warrant planned early delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Obstetric Cholestasis)

options.text: Ursodeoxycholic acid and delivery at 36 weeks gestation
options.rationale: This patient has severe obstetric cholestasis with bile acids 55 micromol/L (above the 40 micromol/L threshold for severe disease). RANZCOG and RCOG guidelines recommend ursodeoxycholic acid for symptom relief and planned delivery at 36 weeks for severe cholestasis (bile acids above 40 micromol/L). The PITCHES trial demonstrated that while ursodeoxycholic acid improves pruritus, it does not reduce perinatal complications, reinforcing the importance of timed delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Obstetric Cholestasis; RCOG Green-top Guideline: Obstetric Cholestasis)

options.text: Dexamethasone for foetal lung maturity and delivery at 34 weeks
options.rationale: Delivery at 34 weeks is earlier than guidelines recommend for this bile acid level. Corticosteroids for foetal lung maturity should be administered before planned delivery at 36 weeks but the delivery itself should not be brought forward to 34 weeks for bile acids of 55 micromol/L. Earlier delivery (34-35 weeks) may be considered for bile acids above 100 micromol/L. (Source: RANZCOG: Obstetric Cholestasis)

explanation.summary: Obstetric cholestasis (intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy) presents with pruritus concentrated on the palms and soles in the third trimester, with elevated serum bile acids. Severity is stratified by bile acid levels: mild (10-39 micromol/L) with delivery at 38-39 weeks, and severe (40 micromol/L or above) with delivery at 36 weeks. The risk of stillbirth increases sharply above 40 micromol/L and correlates with bile acid concentration. Ursodeoxycholic acid improves pruritus but the PITCHES trial showed no reduction in perinatal adverse outcomes, making timed delivery the critical intervention.
key_points: Bile acids above 40 micromol/L classify obstetric cholestasis as severe with delivery recommended at 36 weeks
Bile acids 10-39 micromol/L are mild-moderate with delivery at 38-39 weeks
Ursodeoxycholic acid improves pruritus but the PITCHES trial showed no improvement in perinatal outcomes
Stillbirth risk increases significantly with bile acids above 40 micromol/L
Weekly bile acid monitoring is recommended as levels may fluctuate
explanation.pearls: The PITCHES trial (Lancet 2019) showed that ursodeoxycholic acid at 500 mg twice daily reduced pruritus scores but did not reduce the composite perinatal outcome - planned delivery timing based on bile acid severity remains the key intervention for preventing stillbirth.

subtopic_detail: Cervical insufficiency - rescue cerclage decision
stem: A 24-year-old woman, G2P1 (previous mid-trimester loss at 20 weeks), presents at 18 weeks gestation with a sensation of vaginal pressure. She has no contractions, no bleeding, and no history of ruptured membranes. A speculum examination shows no pooling of liquor and the cervix appears visually shortened. She is afebrile.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 78, Temp 36.5 C
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal ultrasound: cervical length 12 mm with funnelling to the external os. Membranes visible at the external os but not bulging beyond it. No uterine contractions on tocography. Foetal heart rate 150 bpm with normal anatomy scan. CRP 4 mg/L (normal). White cell count 9.8 x10^9/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Vaginal progesterone pessary alone with serial cervical lengths
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone may be offered for a short cervix detected on screening ultrasound in a woman without a history of mid-trimester loss. However, this woman has a cervical length of 12 mm with funnelling, visible membranes at the os, and a prior mid-trimester loss, meeting criteria for rescue cerclage. Progesterone alone is insufficient. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Bed rest and tocolysis with repeat assessment in 1 week
options.rationale: Bed rest has not been shown to prevent preterm birth and may increase thromboembolic risk. With a critically short cervix (12 mm), funnelling, and membranes at the os in a woman with a prior mid-trimester loss, delaying intervention by a week risks further cervical change and membrane prolapse. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Oral antibiotics and expectant management until viability
options.rationale: There is no evidence of infection (normal CRP and white cell count, afebrile). Antibiotics are indicated if cerclage is performed or if there is clinical evidence of chorioamnionitis. Expectant management with a 12 mm cervix and prior mid-trimester loss carries a high risk of recurrence. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Induction of labour as pregnancy is not viable at 18 weeks
options.rationale: Termination is not indicated for cervical insufficiency. This is a wanted pregnancy with a viable foetus and no signs of infection. Rescue cerclage can extend the pregnancy significantly, with reported average prolongation of 5-9 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Emergency rescue cerclage after excluding ruptured membranes
options.rationale: This woman meets criteria for rescue (emergency) cerclage: cervical length less than 25 mm with funnelling and visible membranes at the os, prior mid-trimester loss consistent with cervical insufficiency, no evidence of infection or ruptured membranes, and gestational age between 16 and 24 weeks. After confirming intact membranes (negative pooling on speculum, negative ferning and IGFBP-1), cerclage placement can prolong the pregnancy. Perioperative antibiotics and a short course of indomethacin are typically administered. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency; Cochrane Review: Cerclage)

explanation.summary: Cervical insufficiency classically presents with painless cervical dilatation and shortening in the second trimester, often progressing to membrane prolapse and mid-trimester loss. A history of prior mid-trimester loss is the strongest predictor. Cerclage types include: history-indicated (placed at 12-14 weeks in women with characteristic history), ultrasound-indicated (cervix less than 25 mm on surveillance), and rescue (advanced cervical change with visible membranes). Rescue cerclage is technically challenging but can prolong pregnancy by a median of 5-9 weeks. Contraindications include chorioamnionitis, ruptured membranes, and active vaginal bleeding.
key_points: Rescue cerclage is indicated when cervical length is critically short with funnelling and visible membranes at the os
Exclusion of ruptured membranes and chorioamnionitis is mandatory before cerclage placement
Rescue cerclage prolongs pregnancy by a median of 5-9 weeks compared with expectant management
Perioperative indomethacin (48 hours) and antibiotics are standard adjunctive therapy
History-indicated cerclage at 12-14 weeks would have been appropriate in this woman given her prior mid-trimester loss
explanation.pearls: A woman with one prior mid-trimester loss should have cervical length surveillance from 14 weeks in the next pregnancy - ultrasound-indicated cerclage at cervical length less than 25 mm prevents approximately 30% of preterm births in this high-risk group.

subtopic_detail: Placental abruption - concealed haemorrhage management
stem: A 30-year-old woman at 33 weeks gestation presents with sudden onset severe constant abdominal pain. She has no vaginal bleeding. She has a history of chronic hypertension on labetalol. Her uterus is tense and tender on palpation, described as 'woody hard'. The foetal heart rate tracing shows recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 150/95 mmHg, HR 115, RR 22, SpO2 98%, Temp 36.8 C
data_table.Examination: Uterus tense, tender, and firm ('woody'). Fundal height 35 cm (above dates). No visible vaginal bleeding on speculum. Cervix closed.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 95 g/L (was 118 g/L at booking), platelets 110 x10^9/L, fibrinogen 1.8 g/L (low), INR 1.3, APTT 38 s. Kleihauer test: positive.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Emergency caesarean section with concurrent resuscitation
options.rationale: This presentation is consistent with a severe concealed placental abruption: sudden constant abdominal pain, woody hard uterus, absence of vaginal bleeding but dropping haemoglobin, consumptive coagulopathy (low fibrinogen, low platelets, raised INR), and foetal compromise (late decelerations with reduced variability). Emergency caesarean section is indicated for foetal distress at a viable gestation. Concurrent resuscitation with packed red cells, cryoprecipitate (fibrinogen target above 2 g/L), and fresh frozen plasma should be initiated. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage; Queensland Clinical Guideline: APH)

options.text: Tocolysis with nifedipine and antenatal corticosteroids
options.rationale: Tocolysis is absolutely contraindicated in placental abruption. The uterine contractions are a response to the retroplacental haemorrhage and suppressing them may worsen haemorrhage. The foetus is showing signs of acute compromise requiring delivery, not expectant management. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Amniotomy and oxytocin augmentation for vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Amniotomy and induction may be considered in a mild abruption with a reassuring foetal heart trace and favourable cervix. This foetus has recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability (pathological trace) and the cervix is closed, making vaginal delivery unsafe and impractical. Emergency caesarean is required. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound to confirm retroplacental clot first
options.rationale: Ultrasound has a sensitivity of only 25-50% for detecting retroplacental haemorrhage and should not delay delivery when the clinical diagnosis is clear. The combination of sudden severe pain, woody uterus, dropping haemoglobin, coagulopathy, and foetal distress is sufficient for a clinical diagnosis of severe abruption. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection and observe for 4 hours
options.rationale: While magnesium sulfate for foetal neuroprotection is indicated before preterm delivery under 30 weeks, this foetus is 33 weeks and is acutely compromised. The priority is immediate delivery to prevent intrauterine foetal death. A 4-hour observation period risks foetal demise and worsening maternal coagulopathy. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: APH)

explanation.summary: Concealed placental abruption is one of the most dangerous obstetric emergencies because the absence of vaginal bleeding can delay diagnosis. The retroplacental haemorrhage tracks into the myometrium (Couvelaire uterus), causing the characteristic woody hard tender uterus. Disseminated intravascular coagulation develops in 10-20% of severe abruptions, particularly concealed ones, because thromboplastin from the damaged placenta enters the maternal circulation. Fibrinogen below 2 g/L is a critical marker. Chronic hypertension is the strongest risk factor for abruption.
key_points: Concealed abruption has no visible vaginal bleeding but the retroplacental haemorrhage causes a tense, woody uterus
Ultrasound sensitivity for retroplacental clot is only 25-50% - a negative scan does not exclude abruption
DIC develops in 10-20% of severe abruptions - fibrinogen below 2 g/L requires urgent cryoprecipitate replacement
Tocolysis is absolutely contraindicated in placental abruption
Chronic hypertension is the strongest risk factor for abruption
explanation.pearls: Fibrinogen is the earliest coagulation factor to become depleted in abruption-associated DIC - a level below 2 g/L in the setting of abruption requires 10 units of cryoprecipitate and predicts a need for massive transfusion protocol activation.

subtopic_detail: Shoulder dystocia - HELPERR manoeuvre sequence
stem: You are the registrar attending a vaginal delivery. A 32-year-old woman, G2P1, has had a normal first stage and is now in the second stage. The foetal head delivers but immediately retracts against the perineum (turtle sign). Gentle downward traction on the head does not deliver the anterior shoulder. An assistant has already called for help.

data_table.Examination: Foetal head delivered, chin tight against perineum (turtle sign). Anterior shoulder impacted behind the symphysis pubis. Mother in lithotomy position.

lead_in: After calling for help and performing an episiotomy, what is the most appropriate next manoeuvre?

options.text: Fundal pressure to push the anterior shoulder past the pubis
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated in shoulder dystocia as it further impacts the anterior shoulder against the symphysis pubis and increases the risk of brachial plexus injury, uterine rupture, and foetal injury. This is consistently listed as a dangerous action in all shoulder dystocia guidelines. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia; RCOG Green-top Guideline: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: McRoberts position with directed suprapubic pressure applied
options.rationale: The McRoberts manoeuvre (hyperflexion of the maternal hips onto the abdomen) combined with suprapubic pressure is the recommended first-line intervention for shoulder dystocia. It flattens the lumbar lordosis, rotates the pelvis, and increases the functional AP diameter of the pelvic outlet. Combined with suprapubic pressure (applied by an assistant from above the pubis to push the anterior shoulder towards the foetal chest), it resolves approximately 60% of shoulder dystocia cases. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia; RCOG Green-top Guideline: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Delivery of the posterior arm by sweeping across the chest
options.rationale: Delivery of the posterior arm is an effective internal manoeuvre but is a second-line step after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure have been tried. It requires reaching into the vagina to flex and sweep the posterior arm across the foetal chest. While effective, it carries a higher risk of humeral fracture than external manoeuvres. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Rubin II rotational manoeuvre to disimpact the anterior shoulder
options.rationale: The Rubin II manoeuvre (pressure on the posterior aspect of the anterior shoulder to rotate it into an oblique diameter) is an internal rotational manoeuvre that is tried after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure fail. It is effective but is not the first step. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Move to all-fours position immediately (Gaskin manoeuvre)
options.rationale: The all-fours (Gaskin) manoeuvre changes the pelvic dimensions and allows gravity to assist delivery. It is an effective option but is difficult to perform with epidural anaesthesia and is generally considered after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure have failed. In the HELPERR mnemonic it follows external and internal manoeuvres. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia occurs when the foetal anterior shoulder impacts behind the maternal symphysis pubis after delivery of the head. The turtle sign (retraction of the delivered head against the perineum) is the hallmark. The HELPERR mnemonic guides the stepwise response: Help, Episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter (internal manoeuvres), Remove posterior arm, Roll (all-fours). McRoberts with suprapubic pressure resolves approximately 60% of cases and should always be attempted first. Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated.
key_points: McRoberts manoeuvre with suprapubic pressure is first-line and resolves approximately 60% of cases
Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated - it worsens impaction and increases brachial plexus injury risk
Suprapubic pressure pushes the anterior shoulder towards the foetal chest (not away from it)
Internal manoeuvres (Rubin II, Wood screw, posterior arm delivery) are second-line after McRoberts fails
Brachial plexus injury (Erb palsy) occurs in 5-15% of shoulder dystocia, with most resolving within 12 months
explanation.pearls: Suprapubic pressure in shoulder dystocia is applied from above the pubic symphysis directed posteriorly and laterally to push the anterior shoulder towards the foetal chest and under the symphysis - it must never be confused with fundal pressure, which worsens impaction.

subtopic_detail: Breech at term - ECV eligibility and management
stem: A 37-year-old woman, G3P2, is found to have a frank breech presentation at her 36-week antenatal visit. Her two previous deliveries were uncomplicated vaginal births at term. She has a normal amniotic fluid index, anterior placenta in the upper segment, and no uterine anomalies on prior ultrasound. She would prefer a vaginal birth if possible.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 115/70 mmHg, HR 76
data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound: frank breech, estimated foetal weight 2800 g, AFI 14 cm, anterior upper segment placenta, no foetal anomalies. CTG: reactive.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Elective caesarean section at 39 weeks for breech presentation
options.rationale: While elective caesarean section is an option for breech presentation, external cephalic version should be offered first to women without contraindications. The Term Breech Trial influenced practice toward caesarean section, but ECV at 37 weeks allows many women to avoid surgery. This woman is an ideal ECV candidate. (Source: RANZCOG: Breech Presentation; Cochrane Review: ECV)

options.text: Planned vaginal breech delivery at an experienced unit
options.rationale: Planned vaginal breech delivery may be offered at centres with experienced practitioners, but ECV should be attempted first as it has a success rate of 50-60% and avoids the risks of both breech vaginal delivery and caesarean section. Proceeding directly to vaginal breech delivery without attempting ECV misses an opportunity for cephalic presentation. (Source: RANZCOG: Breech Presentation)

options.text: External cephalic version at 37 weeks with tocolysis
options.rationale: ECV at 37 weeks is the recommended first-line management for uncomplicated breech presentation in a multiparous woman. This woman has no contraindications (normal AFI, no placenta praevia, no uterine scar, reactive CTG). A subcutaneous terbutaline injection as a tocolytic increases success rates. ECV in multiparous women has a success rate of 60-70%. If successful, she can plan for a normal vaginal birth. (Source: RANZCOG: Breech Presentation; Cochrane Review: ECV)

options.text: Moxibustion and postural exercises until 38 weeks then review
options.rationale: Moxibustion (burning artemisia near acupuncture point BL67) and postural techniques (knee-chest position) have limited evidence for effectiveness in turning a breech baby. Delaying ECV to 38 weeks reduces the success rate as the foetus has less room to turn. RANZCOG recommends ECV at 37 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Breech Presentation)

options.text: Observation until 39 weeks as spontaneous version may occur
options.rationale: While spontaneous version can occur, the probability decreases after 36 weeks, particularly in a frank breech. Only 5-10% of breech presentations will spontaneously convert to cephalic after 36 weeks. Waiting until 39 weeks leaves insufficient time for ECV if spontaneous version does not occur, often resulting in a caesarean section. (Source: RANZCOG: Breech Presentation)

explanation.summary: External cephalic version (ECV) is the recommended first-line intervention for breech presentation at term. RANZCOG recommends offering ECV from 37 weeks gestation. Success rates are higher in multiparous women (60-70%) compared with primiparous women (40-50%). Contraindications include placenta praevia, uterine scar (relative), ruptured membranes, non-reassuring CTG, and conditions requiring caesarean delivery. Tocolysis with subcutaneous terbutaline or nifedipine improves success rates. Anti-D should be administered to Rh-negative women after ECV.
key_points: ECV at 37 weeks is first-line management for uncomplicated breech presentation
ECV success rates: 60-70% in multiparous women, 40-50% in primiparous women
Tocolysis with terbutaline or nifedipine improves ECV success rates
Contraindications: placenta praevia, ruptured membranes, non-reassuring CTG, antepartum haemorrhage
Anti-D immunoglobulin is required for Rh-negative women after ECV
explanation.pearls: ECV success rates are highest when performed at 37 weeks in a multiparous woman with a normal AFI, non-engaged presenting part, and a posterior placenta - the success rate drops approximately 10% for each week delayed beyond 37 weeks.

subtopic_detail: Third and fourth degree perineal tear - recognition and repair
stem: You are the registrar assessing a primiparous woman who has just had a spontaneous vaginal delivery of a 4.1 kg baby after a prolonged second stage. An episiotomy was not performed. On perineal examination under good lighting, you identify a tear involving the vaginal mucosa, perineal skin, perineal muscles, and you can see the fibres of the external anal sphincter. The internal anal sphincter appears intact on examination, and the anal mucosa is undamaged.

data_table.Examination: Perineal tear involving vaginal mucosa, perineal skin, perineal body muscles, and the external anal sphincter capsule with visible torn fibres. Internal anal sphincter visually intact. Anal mucosa intact. No active arterial bleeding.

lead_in: What is the correct classification and most appropriate management?

options.text: Second degree tear - repair in the delivery room by midwife
options.rationale: A second degree tear involves perineal muscles but not the anal sphincter complex. This tear extends to the external anal sphincter, making it at least a third degree tear. Misclassification as a second degree tear risks inadequate repair and long-term anal incontinence. (Source: RANZCOG: Third and Fourth Degree Tears; RCOG Green-top Guideline: OASI)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Third and Fourth Degree Tears

options.text: 3a tear - repair with continuous suture technique in the room
options.rationale: While the classification of 3a (less than 50% of EAS torn) may be correct depending on the extent of sphincter involvement, OASI repair should be performed in theatre under regional or general anaesthesia with good lighting, appropriate suture materials, and by a trained practitioner. Repair in the delivery room risks suboptimal outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG: Third and Fourth Degree Tears)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Third and Fourth Degree Tears

options.text: 3b tear - formal repair by a doctor in theatre with PDS sutures
options.rationale: A 3b tear involves more than 50% of the EAS thickness. Depending on the extent of sphincter involvement, this may or may not be the correct sub-classification. However, regardless of whether this is 3a or 3b, the repair should occur in theatre. The key error in this option is specifying PDS (a monofilament suture) without noting that either PDS or Vicryl are acceptable for sphincter repair. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline: OASI)

options.text: 3a or 3b tear - sphincter repair in theatre with antibiotics
options.rationale: The external anal sphincter is involved but the internal sphincter and anal mucosa are intact, classifying this as a third degree tear (3a if less than 50% EAS, 3b if more than 50%). Repair must be performed in an operating theatre with adequate anaesthesia (regional or general), good lighting, and by a doctor experienced in sphincter repair. Post-repair antibiotics (typically a single dose of a broad-spectrum agent) and laxatives are recommended. Follow-up at 6-12 weeks for continence assessment is essential. (Source: RANZCOG: Third and Fourth Degree Tears; RCOG Green-top Guideline: OASI)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Third and Fourth Degree Tears

options.text: Fourth degree tear - repair under general anaesthesia urgently
options.rationale: A fourth degree tear involves the external and internal anal sphincter AND the anal epithelium. This tear has an intact internal anal sphincter and intact anal mucosa, making it a third degree tear, not a fourth degree. Accurate classification is critical for determining the repair technique and prognosis. (Source: RANZCOG: Third and Fourth Degree Tears)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Third and Fourth Degree Tears

explanation.summary: Obstetric anal sphincter injuries (OASI) are classified into four subtypes: 3a (less than 50% of EAS), 3b (more than 50% of EAS), 3c (EAS plus IAS), and 4th degree (EAS, IAS, and anal epithelium). Accurate classification requires systematic examination under good lighting. All OASI repairs should be performed in theatre by a trained doctor. Risk factors include macrosomia, prolonged second stage, primiparity, and instrumental delivery (particularly forceps). Post-repair management includes antibiotics, laxatives, and continence follow-up at 6-12 weeks.
key_points: Third degree tears are sub-classified: 3a (less than 50% EAS), 3b (more than 50% EAS), 3c (EAS plus IAS)
Fourth degree tear additionally involves the anal epithelium (rectal mucosa)
All OASI must be repaired in theatre with adequate anaesthesia, not in the delivery room
Post-repair: antibiotics, laxatives, physiotherapy referral, and continence review at 6-12 weeks
Risk factors: primiparity, macrosomia (above 4 kg), forceps delivery, prolonged second stage, midline episiotomy
explanation.pearls: Up to 30% of obstetric anal sphincter injuries are missed at the time of delivery - a structured systematic perineal examination (including rectal examination) after every vaginal delivery is essential for early detection and optimal repair.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Third and Fourth Degree Tears

subtopic_detail: Vulvar lichen sclerosus - management and malignancy surveillance
stem: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with a 2-year history of vulvar pruritus and soreness. She has tried over-the-counter antifungal cream without improvement. On examination there are white atrophic plaques involving the labia minora, clitoral hood, and perianal skin in a 'figure-of-eight' distribution. There is partial fusion of the labia minora and burying of the clitoris. No areas of hyperkeratosis or ulceration are seen.

data_table.Examination: White porcelain-like atrophic plaques in figure-of-eight distribution involving labia minora, clitoral hood, and perianal skin. Partial labial fusion. Clitoral burying. No hyperkeratotic, erosive, or ulcerated areas. No lymphadenopathy.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Topical oestrogen cream applied nightly for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Topical oestrogen treats vulvovaginal atrophy but is not the primary treatment for lichen sclerosus. While vulvar atrophy may coexist in postmenopausal women, the characteristic white plaques and architectural changes require potent topical corticosteroid therapy. (Source: RCOG: Vulvar Dermatoses; eTG: Dermatology)

options.text: Topical antifungal and hydrocortisone combination cream
options.rationale: Mild hydrocortisone is insufficient for lichen sclerosus, which requires a potent or superpotent topical corticosteroid. The addition of an antifungal is unnecessary unless there is concurrent candidiasis. This patient has already failed antifungal therapy. (Source: eTG: Dermatology)

options.text: Vulvar biopsy and surgical excision of all affected skin
options.rationale: Surgical excision is not indicated for lichen sclerosus - the condition typically recurs after surgery and excision causes unnecessary morbidity. Biopsy is indicated only if there are areas of hyperkeratosis, ulceration, or clinical suspicion of malignancy. This patient has no atypical features requiring biopsy. (Source: RCOG: Vulvar Dermatoses)

options.text: Superpotent topical corticosteroid and long-term follow-up
options.rationale: This option correctly identifies the need for potent corticosteroid therapy and follow-up. However, Australian guidelines recommend potent (betamethasone dipropionate 0.05%) or superpotent (clobetasol propionate 0.05%) topical corticosteroids, not exclusively superpotent. The critical additional component of this answer is that it does not distinguish from option E below. (Source: eTG: Dermatology)

options.text: Potent topical corticosteroid with annual vulvar review
options.rationale: The first-line treatment for vulvar lichen sclerosus is a potent topical corticosteroid such as betamethasone dipropionate 0.05% or clobetasol propionate 0.05%, applied nightly for 4 weeks, then alternate nights for 4 weeks, then twice weekly as maintenance. Long-term annual vulvar review is essential because lichen sclerosus carries a 3-5% lifetime risk of vulvar squamous cell carcinoma. Any new hyperkeratotic, erosive, or nodular areas should be biopsied. (Source: eTG: Dermatology; RCOG: Vulvar Dermatoses; ISSVD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Vulvar lichen sclerosus is a chronic inflammatory dermatosis with a predilection for genital skin. It has a bimodal age distribution (prepubertal girls and postmenopausal women). The characteristic figure-of-eight distribution around the vulva and perianal skin with white porcelain-like plaques is highly distinctive. First-line treatment is potent topical corticosteroid therapy with a step-down maintenance regimen. The condition is lifelong, requiring ongoing treatment and surveillance. The 3-5% lifetime risk of vulvar squamous cell carcinoma necessitates annual clinical review and biopsy of any new suspicious areas.
key_points: First-line treatment: potent topical corticosteroid (betamethasone or clobetasol) nightly for 4 weeks, then step-down
Figure-of-eight distribution around vulva and perianal skin is the characteristic pattern
3-5% lifetime risk of vulvar squamous cell carcinoma necessitates long-term surveillance
Biopsy is indicated for hyperkeratotic, erosive, pigmented, or nodular areas - not routine uncomplicated disease
Architectural changes (labial fusion, clitoral burying) may be irreversible and can impair sexual function
explanation.pearls: Lichen sclerosus carries a 3-5% lifetime risk of vulvar SCC - the risk is highest in women who do not use maintenance topical corticosteroids, making adherence to long-term twice-weekly application and annual clinical review the key preventive strategy.

subtopic_detail: Menopause - MHT eligibility with cardiovascular considerations
stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with debilitating vasomotor symptoms (8-10 hot flushes daily, severe night sweats disrupting sleep) for the past 18 months since her last menstrual period. She has tried lifestyle modifications and phytoestrogens without benefit. Her BMI is 26 kg/m2. She has no personal history of breast cancer or thromboembolic disease. She is a non-smoker with no hypertension or diabetes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 72
data_table.Investigations: Mammogram (screening): BI-RADS 1 (normal). Cervical screening: negative HPV at age 50. Fasting lipids: total cholesterol 5.4 mmol/L, LDL 3.2 mmol/L, HDL 1.5 mmol/L, triglycerides 1.2 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of her vasomotor symptoms?

options.text: Combined continuous MHT with oral oestradiol and progesterone
options.rationale: This postmenopausal woman (amenorrhoeic for over 12 months) with debilitating vasomotor symptoms that have failed lifestyle measures and phytoestrogens is an ideal candidate for menopausal hormone therapy. She has no contraindications. Combined continuous MHT (oestrogen plus progesterone) is appropriate as she has an intact uterus. MHT commenced within 10 years of menopause or before age 60 has a favourable risk-benefit profile. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society; RANZCOG: Menopausal Hormone Therapy)

options.text: Oral oestradiol alone without progesterone at low dose
options.rationale: Unopposed oestrogen is only appropriate for women who have had a hysterectomy. In women with an intact uterus, oestrogen alone increases the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma. A progestogen must be combined with oestrogen for endometrial protection. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society)

options.text: Gabapentin 300 mg nightly as a non-hormonal first-line agent
options.rationale: Gabapentin is a second-line option for vasomotor symptoms in women who cannot take MHT (e.g. history of breast cancer, VTE). This woman has no contraindications to MHT, which is more effective for vasomotor symptoms. Non-hormonal options should be reserved for women with genuine contraindications. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society)

options.text: Tibolone daily as it avoids the need for added progesterone
options.rationale: Tibolone is an alternative to conventional MHT that has oestrogenic, progestogenic, and androgenic activity. However, it is not first-line due to its higher thromboembolic risk profile compared with transdermal MHT and an increased breast cancer risk in the LIBERATE trial in breast cancer survivors. Standard oestrogen-progesterone MHT is preferred as first-line. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society)

options.text: Venlafaxine extended-release as primary vasomotor treatment
options.rationale: Venlafaxine is an effective non-hormonal option for hot flushes but is less effective than MHT and carries risks of discontinuation syndrome and serotonergic side effects. It is second-line, appropriate when MHT is contraindicated. This woman has no contraindications to MHT. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society)

explanation.summary: Menopausal hormone therapy is the most effective treatment for vasomotor symptoms and is first-line when symptoms are moderate to severe and there are no contraindications. The 'window of opportunity' concept supports initiating MHT within 10 years of menopause or before age 60, when the cardiovascular benefits outweigh risks. Combined continuous MHT (oestrogen plus progesterone) is used for postmenopausal women with an intact uterus. Transdermal oestradiol with micronised progesterone has the lowest thromboembolic risk. Annual review with reassessment of benefits and risks is recommended.
key_points: MHT is most effective for vasomotor symptoms and is first-line in the absence of contraindications
Combined MHT (oestrogen plus progesterone) is required for women with an intact uterus
The window of opportunity: initiation within 10 years of menopause or before age 60 has favourable risk-benefit
Transdermal oestradiol with micronised progesterone has the lowest VTE and stroke risk
Contraindications: current breast cancer, active VTE, unexplained vaginal bleeding, active liver disease
explanation.pearls: Transdermal oestradiol (patch or gel) with oral micronised progesterone has near-zero additional VTE risk compared with oral conjugated equine oestrogens, which double VTE risk - the route of oestrogen administration is the key modifiable risk factor.

subtopic_detail: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy - surgical approach
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset severe left iliac fossa pain and vaginal spotting. Her last menstrual period was 7 weeks ago and a urine pregnancy test is positive. She has a history of a previous right salpingectomy for ectopic pregnancy. On examination she is pale with generalised abdominal tenderness and guarding, particularly in the left lower quadrant.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 85/55 mmHg, HR 128, RR 22, SpO2 99%, Temp 36.4 C
data_table.Examination: Pale, diaphoretic. Abdomen: generalised tenderness with guarding, worst in the left iliac fossa. Cervical excitation tenderness on bimanual. Left adnexal mass palpable.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 g/L, beta-hCG 8500 IU/L. FAST ultrasound: free fluid in the Pouch of Douglas and Morrison's pouch.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: IM methotrexate with serial beta-hCG monitoring
options.rationale: Methotrexate is contraindicated in a haemodynamically unstable patient with evidence of rupture. Medical management is reserved for stable patients with unruptured ectopic pregnancies meeting specific criteria (beta-hCG below 5000 IU/L, no foetal cardiac activity, size below 35 mm). (Source: RANZCOG: Ectopic Pregnancy; eTG: Obstetric)

options.text: Emergency laparotomy with left salpingectomy
options.rationale: This patient is haemodynamically unstable with signs of ruptured ectopic pregnancy (hypotension, tachycardia, free fluid in two compartments, Hb 78 g/L). Emergency surgery is required. Left salpingectomy (not salpingotomy) is preferred because she already has only one tube remaining and a ruptured tube is unlikely to function normally. In haemodynamic instability, laparotomy is generally faster than laparoscopy for definitive haemorrhage control. (Source: RANZCOG: Ectopic Pregnancy; RCOG Green-top Guideline: Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Laparoscopic left salpingotomy to preserve fertility
options.rationale: Salpingotomy (tube-conserving surgery) is considered when the patient wishes to preserve fertility and has a contralateral damaged or absent tube. However, this patient is haemodynamically unstable - the priority is rapid haemorrhage control. Laparotomy is preferred over laparoscopy in shocked patients. Additionally, a ruptured tube is unlikely to function normally even after salpingotomy. (Source: RANZCOG: Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Fluid resuscitation and repeat beta-hCG in 48 hours
options.rationale: Expectant management with serial beta-hCG monitoring is only appropriate for stable patients with a pregnancy of unknown location and declining beta-hCG. This patient has a ruptured ectopic with haemodynamic compromise requiring emergency surgery. Delay risks cardiovascular collapse. (Source: RANZCOG: Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Interventional radiology uterine artery embolisation
options.rationale: Uterine artery embolisation is not a standard treatment for ruptured ectopic pregnancy. The bleeding source is the fallopian tube (supplied by tubal and ovarian branches), not the uterine artery. Surgical intervention is the definitive treatment for ruptured ectopic pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG: Ectopic Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate surgical intervention. Haemodynamic instability with free peritoneal fluid mandates emergency surgery - laparotomy is preferred in unstable patients as it provides faster access and haemorrhage control. Salpingectomy is the standard procedure for a ruptured tube. Salpingotomy (tube-conserving surgery) may be considered in haemodynamically stable patients when the contralateral tube is absent or damaged, but a ruptured tube rarely functions normally. This patient's history of a previous right salpingectomy means she will have no tubes after this surgery and will require IVF for future pregnancies.
key_points: Haemodynamic instability in ruptured ectopic mandates emergency laparotomy, not laparoscopy
Salpingectomy is standard for ruptured ectopic pregnancy
Methotrexate is absolutely contraindicated in haemodynamically unstable patients
A woman with bilateral salpingectomy will require IVF for future pregnancies
Ectopic pregnancy risk increases 10-fold after a previous ectopic
explanation.pearls: In haemodynamically unstable ruptured ectopic pregnancy, laparotomy allows faster peritoneal entry and haemorrhage control than laparoscopy - the mean time from skin incision to haemostasis is 5-10 minutes shorter, which can be critical in a patient with an Hb below 80 g/L.

subtopic_detail: Gestational trophoblastic disease - post-molar hCG surveillance
stem: A 25-year-old woman had an uneventful suction evacuation for a complete hydatidiform mole confirmed on histopathology 8 weeks ago. Her initial post-evacuation beta-hCG was 85,000 IU/L. Weekly beta-hCG levels have been declining steadily: 42,000, 18,000, 7,500, 3,200, 1,400, 650, 320, 280. The last two levels at weeks 7 and 8 show a plateau (320 to 280 IU/L over 3 weekly readings).

data_table.Investigations: Serial beta-hCG: Week 1: 42,000 IU/L, Week 2: 18,000, Week 3: 7,500, Week 4: 3,200, Week 5: 1,400, Week 6: 650, Week 7: 320, Week 8: 280 IU/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue weekly beta-hCG monitoring for another 4 weeks
options.rationale: A plateauing beta-hCG (less than 10% decline over 3 consecutive weekly values) after molar evacuation meets the FIGO criteria for gestational trophoblastic neoplasia. Continued observation risks disease progression and metastasis. Referral to a GTD centre for treatment is required. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease; FIGO GTN Staging)

options.text: Repeat suction evacuation of the uterus under ultrasound guidance
options.rationale: A second evacuation increases the risk of uterine perforation and Asherman syndrome and is not recommended for persistent GTN. The plateau in beta-hCG indicates persistent trophoblastic disease requiring chemotherapy, not further surgery. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)

options.text: Oral combined contraceptive pill to suppress gonadotrophins
options.rationale: The oral contraceptive pill does not treat GTN. While reliable contraception is important during hCG surveillance (to prevent a new pregnancy confounding monitoring), the OCP does not address persistent trophoblastic disease. Contraception advice is an adjunct, not a treatment. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)

options.text: Hysterectomy for definitive treatment of persistent mole
options.rationale: Hysterectomy may be offered to women who have completed their family with localised disease, but is not first-line for a 25-year-old with fertility wishes. Chemotherapy is highly effective for GTN with cure rates exceeding 95% for low-risk disease. (Source: FIGO GTN Staging)

options.text: Referral to GTD centre for FIGO staging and chemotherapy
options.rationale: The plateauing beta-hCG over 3 consecutive weekly values meets FIGO criteria for gestational trophoblastic neoplasia. Referral to a specialist GTD centre (in Australia: Monash, Royal Women's, or Mater Brisbane) is required for staging investigations (CXR, pelvic ultrasound, CT if indicated) and FIGO prognostic scoring. Low-risk GTN (FIGO score 0-6) is treated with single-agent methotrexate with cure rates exceeding 95%. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease; FIGO GTN Staging)

explanation.summary: Gestational trophoblastic neoplasia (GTN) is diagnosed after molar evacuation when beta-hCG plateaus (less than 10% decline over 3 consecutive weekly values), rises over 2 consecutive weeks, remains detectable for more than 6 months, or histologically confirmed choriocarcinoma or PSTT is present. Australia has three specialised GTD centres. FIGO scoring combines age, antecedent pregnancy, interval, pre-treatment hCG, tumour size, metastatic sites, number of metastases, and prior failed chemotherapy to stratify risk. Low-risk GTN is cured in over 95% of cases with single-agent methotrexate.
key_points: GTN diagnosis: beta-hCG plateau (less than 10% decline over 3 weekly values), rise over 2 weeks, or persistence beyond 6 months
Referral to a specialist GTD centre is mandatory for FIGO staging and treatment
Low-risk GTN (FIGO score 0-6) is treated with single-agent methotrexate with over 95% cure rate
Reliable contraception is required during hCG surveillance to prevent confounding by a new pregnancy
Post-treatment hCG surveillance continues for 12 months after normalisation for complete mole
explanation.pearls: After complete molar evacuation, the FIGO criterion for GTN diagnosis is a beta-hCG plateau of less than 10% decline across 3 consecutive weekly measurements - this triggers referral to a GTD centre, not continued observation.

subtopic_detail: Perimortem caesarean section - timing decision
stem: A 32-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation collapses in the hospital corridor. She is found to be pulseless and apnoeic. The cardiac arrest team begins CPR. The uterine fundus is palpable above the umbilicus. After 4 minutes of high-quality CPR with adrenaline administered, there is no return of spontaneous circulation.

data_table.Vital signs: Cardiac arrest. No pulse. No spontaneous breathing. Uterine fundus palpable 4 cm above the umbilicus (consistent with approximately 34 weeks).

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue CPR for a further 10 minutes before considering surgery
options.rationale: Delaying perimortem caesarean section beyond 5 minutes of cardiac arrest reduces both maternal and foetal survival. The gravid uterus above 20 weeks compresses the inferior vena cava and aorta, reducing venous return and cardiac output during CPR by up to 30%. Delivery of the foetus and placenta improves maternal haemodynamics. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline: Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy; RANZCOG: Maternal Collapse)
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline: Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy

options.text: Left lateral tilt and continue CPR until ROSC is achieved
options.rationale: Left lateral tilt (or manual left uterine displacement) should be applied from the start of CPR but is a temporising measure. If ROSC is not achieved within 4-5 minutes, perimortem caesarean section must be performed. CPR effectiveness is significantly impaired by aortocaval compression even with optimal tilt. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline: Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy)
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline: Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy

options.text: Transfer to theatre for caesarean section under sterile conditions
options.rationale: Perimortem caesarean section should be performed at the point of arrest - do not move the patient to theatre. Transfer wastes critical minutes and interrupts CPR. The procedure is performed with whatever equipment is available, prioritising speed. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline: Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy)
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline: Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy

options.text: Perimortem caesarean section performed at the point of arrest
options.rationale: Perimortem caesarean section should be commenced by 4 minutes and the baby delivered by 5 minutes of maternal cardiac arrest when ROSC has not been achieved. The primary purpose is to improve maternal resuscitation by relieving aortocaval compression. Foetal survival is a secondary benefit. The procedure is performed at the point of arrest with a vertical midline incision, without waiting for sterile conditions or an operating theatre. CPR continues throughout. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline: Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy; RANZCOG: Maternal Collapse)
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline: Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy

options.text: Bedside ultrasound to confirm foetal viability before proceeding
options.rationale: Ultrasound to check foetal viability should not delay perimortem caesarean section. The primary indication for the procedure is maternal resuscitation (relief of aortocaval compression), not foetal delivery. Even if the foetus is already dead, removing the gravid uterus improves maternal cardiac output during CPR. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline: Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy)
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline: Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy

explanation.summary: Perimortem caesarean section is performed during maternal cardiac arrest when ROSC has not been achieved within 4-5 minutes and the uterine fundus is at or above the umbilicus (approximately 20 weeks). The primary purpose is maternal - relieving aortocaval compression improves venous return and cardiac output during CPR by up to 30%. The decision to perform the procedure should be made by 4 minutes, with delivery by 5 minutes. The procedure is performed at the point of arrest, not in an operating theatre. CPR continues throughout and after delivery.
key_points: Perimortem caesarean section decision by 4 minutes, delivery by 5 minutes of maternal cardiac arrest
Primary indication is maternal resuscitation, not foetal delivery
Perform at the point of arrest - do not transfer to theatre
Aortocaval compression reduces cardiac output by up to 30% in the supine gravid patient after 20 weeks
CPR must continue during and after the procedure - ROSC may occur after delivery
explanation.pearls: Perimortem caesarean section is a MATERNAL resuscitation procedure, not a foetal rescue operation - even when the foetus is known to be dead, removing the gravid uterus after 20 weeks improves venous return and may achieve ROSC where CPR alone failed.

sources.label: ANZCOR Guideline: Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Ovarian cyst in pregnancy - management by trimester
stem: A 29-year-old woman at 14 weeks gestation is found to have a 7 cm simple unilocular ovarian cyst on her dating scan. She is asymptomatic with no pain, nausea, or fever. A repeat ultrasound at 14 weeks confirms the cyst is persistent, simple (anechoic, thin-walled, no solid components, no internal septations), measuring 7.2 cm on the left ovary. CA-125 is 45 U/mL.

data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound 8 weeks: 6.8 cm simple left ovarian cyst. Ultrasound 14 weeks: 7.2 cm simple left ovarian cyst, unchanged morphology. No ascites. CA-125: 45 U/mL (note: CA-125 is physiologically elevated in pregnancy, especially first trimester).

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Urgent laparotomy for ovarian cyst removal this week
options.rationale: Urgent surgical intervention is not indicated for an asymptomatic simple cyst without concerning features. Laparotomy carries higher morbidity than laparoscopy and is not the preferred approach for benign-appearing cysts. Surgery, if required, can be performed electively. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Masses in Pregnancy; RCOG Green-top Guideline: Adnexal Masses)

options.text: Observation with repeat ultrasound at 18-20 weeks
options.rationale: Observation is reasonable for small simple cysts, but a persistent simple cyst of 7 cm or larger has a higher risk of torsion, rupture, or obstruction during pregnancy and labour. At 14 weeks (early second trimester), the optimal surgical window is now, as the miscarriage risk from surgery is lowest between 14-18 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Masses in Pregnancy)

options.text: Laparoscopic cystectomy between 14 and 18 weeks gestation
options.rationale: A persistent simple ovarian cyst of 7 cm or larger in pregnancy warrants surgical consideration due to the risk of torsion (10-15% for cysts above 5 cm), rupture, and obstructed labour. The optimal window for elective surgery in pregnancy is 14-18 weeks - after organogenesis is complete (reducing teratogenic risk from anaesthesia) and before the uterus is too large for laparoscopic access. Laparoscopy is preferred over laparotomy for benign-appearing cysts. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Masses in Pregnancy; RCOG Green-top Guideline: Adnexal Masses)

options.text: CT pelvis to better characterise the cyst before deciding
options.rationale: CT pelvis involves ionising radiation and should be avoided in pregnancy unless absolutely necessary. MRI without gadolinium is the preferred cross-sectional imaging modality in pregnancy if further characterisation is needed beyond ultrasound. However, this cyst is simple and well-characterised on ultrasound. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Masses in Pregnancy)

options.text: Ultrasound-guided aspiration of the cyst contents
