options.rationale: Aspiration of ovarian cysts has a high recurrence rate (up to 70%) and does not provide histological diagnosis. It is not recommended as definitive management. Surgical excision (cystectomy) is preferred for persistent large cysts requiring intervention. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline: Adnexal Masses)

explanation.summary: Adnexal masses are found in 1-2% of pregnancies, most commonly corpus luteum cysts that resolve by 16 weeks. Persistent cysts above 5-6 cm carry a significant risk of torsion (10-15%), and those above 7 cm are at additional risk of obstructing labour. The optimal surgical window in pregnancy is 14-18 weeks gestation, after organogenesis and before the uterus limits laparoscopic access. Simple cysts with benign ultrasound features can be managed with laparoscopic cystectomy. CA-125 is unreliable in pregnancy as it is physiologically elevated in the first trimester.
key_points: Persistent ovarian cysts above 5-6 cm in pregnancy carry a 10-15% risk of torsion
Optimal surgical window in pregnancy is 14-18 weeks gestation
Laparoscopy is safe in pregnancy and preferred over laparotomy for benign-appearing cysts
CA-125 is physiologically elevated in pregnancy and unreliable for malignancy risk assessment
MRI without gadolinium is the preferred cross-sectional imaging if ultrasound is insufficient
explanation.pearls: CA-125 levels are physiologically elevated in the first trimester of pregnancy (up to 100 U/mL or higher) due to decidual production - a mildly elevated CA-125 in early pregnancy should not raise concern for malignancy in the context of a simple-appearing ovarian cyst.

subtopic_detail: Fibroids in pregnancy - red degeneration management
stem: A 33-year-old woman at 22 weeks gestation presents with acute onset right-sided abdominal pain, nausea, and low-grade fever. She has a known 8 cm intramural uterine fibroid documented on her dating scan. The pain is localised to the right flank and is constant. She has no vaginal bleeding or contractions.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 98, BP 120/75 mmHg, Temp 37.8 C, RR 18
data_table.Examination: Localised tenderness over the right lateral aspect of the uterus corresponding to the known fibroid location. The fibroid is exquisitely tender on palpation. No peritoneal signs. Cervix closed and long on digital examination.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 13.2 x10^9/L, CRP 42 mg/L. Urinalysis: no leucocytes, no nitrites. Ultrasound: 8.5 cm intramural fibroid with heterogeneous echotexture suggesting internal haemorrhage. Foetus viable, normal amniotic fluid.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Analgesia, hydration, and supportive care with monitoring
options.rationale: Red (carneous) degeneration of a fibroid in pregnancy is caused by haemorrhagic infarction of the fibroid due to outgrowing its blood supply. Management is conservative: rest, hydration, and analgesia (paracetamol as first-line, short courses of NSAIDs may be used cautiously before 30 weeks). The pain typically resolves within 7-14 days. Surgical intervention (myomectomy) in pregnancy carries high risks of haemorrhage and preterm labour and is reserved for failure of medical management. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy; eTG: Obstetric)

options.text: Urgent myomectomy to remove the degenerating fibroid
options.rationale: Myomectomy during pregnancy carries a very high risk of uncontrollable haemorrhage due to the increased uterine vascularity. It also increases the risk of preterm labour and emergency hysterectomy. Surgery is reserved for rare complications such as torsion of a pedunculated fibroid or refractory pain not responding to 2-3 weeks of conservative management. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)

options.text: IV antibiotics for suspected infected fibroid or abscess
options.rationale: Infected fibroids are extremely rare. The mild leucocytosis and low-grade fever in this context are consistent with the inflammatory response of red degeneration, not infection. There is no indication for antibiotics. The ultrasound shows internal haemorrhage consistent with degeneration, not an abscess collection. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)

options.text: Uterine artery embolisation to infarct the fibroid
options.rationale: Uterine artery embolisation is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy as it risks placental infarction, foetal death, and uterine necrosis. It is used for symptomatic fibroids in non-pregnant women who wish to avoid surgery. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)

options.text: Delivery by caesarean section to relieve the complication
options.rationale: At 22 weeks gestation the foetus is at the lower limit of viability and delivery would result in extreme prematurity with very high morbidity and mortality. Red degeneration is a self-limiting condition that resolves with conservative management. Delivery is not indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Red (carneous) degeneration of a uterine fibroid typically occurs in the second and early third trimester when the rapidly growing fibroid outstrips its blood supply, causing haemorrhagic infarction. The classic presentation is acute localised uterine pain over the fibroid, low-grade fever, and mild leucocytosis. Ultrasound may show heterogeneous echotexture within the fibroid. Management is conservative with rest, hydration, and analgesia. The condition is self-limiting over 7-14 days. Surgical intervention is reserved for rare complications and carries significant risks during pregnancy.
key_points: Red degeneration occurs in the second trimester when the fibroid outgrows its blood supply
Conservative management (analgesia, hydration, rest) is first-line with resolution in 7-14 days
Myomectomy in pregnancy carries very high haemorrhage risk and is reserved for refractory cases
Low-grade fever and leucocytosis are expected features of the inflammatory response, not infection
Uterine artery embolisation is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Paracetamol is first-line analgesia for red degeneration in pregnancy, but short courses of indomethacin (48-72 hours) may be used before 30 weeks if paracetamol is insufficient - after 30 weeks, NSAIDs risk premature ductal closure and should be avoided.

subtopic_detail: Congenital long QT syndrome - neonatal management
stem: A 5-day-old term neonate is found to have a heart rate of 45 bpm during routine monitoring in the postnatal ward. An ECG shows 2:1 atrioventricular block with a ventricular rate of 48 bpm. The atrial rate is 96 bpm. The QTc interval on the ECG is measured at 620 ms. There is no maternal history of anti-Ro or anti-La antibodies. The mother has no history of syncope or cardiac arrest. The neonate is haemodynamically stable with good perfusion.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 48 bpm, BP 58/35 mmHg (normal for neonate), SpO2 96%, Temp 36.8 C
data_table.Examination: Well-perfused. No murmur. Liver not enlarged. Feeding well.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: atrial rate 96 bpm, ventricular rate 48 bpm. 2:1 AV block. QTc 620 ms (Bazett formula). Alternating T-wave morphology (T-wave alternans). Echocardiogram: structurally normal heart. Maternal anti-Ro/anti-La antibodies: negative.

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Autoimmune congenital heart block - isoprenaline infusion
options.rationale: Autoimmune congenital heart block from maternal anti-Ro/anti-La antibodies causes isolated complete heart block but does not prolong the QTc interval. Maternal antibodies are negative in this case. The combination of 2:1 AV block, markedly prolonged QTc (620 ms), and T-wave alternans is diagnostic of congenital long QT syndrome, not autoimmune heart block. (Source: RCH CPG: Cardiac Arrhythmias; CSANZ: Inherited Cardiac Conditions)

options.text: Neonatal myocarditis - IV immunoglobulin and supportive care
options.rationale: Neonatal myocarditis typically presents with heart failure, poor feeding, and ventricular dysfunction on echocardiography. This neonate has a structurally and functionally normal heart. The markedly prolonged QTc with 2:1 AV block and T-wave alternans is characteristic of channelopathy, not myocarditis. (Source: RCH CPG: Cardiac Arrhythmias)

options.text: Electrolyte disturbance - IV calcium and magnesium correction
options.rationale: Hypocalcaemia and hypomagnesaemia can prolong the QTc interval but do not typically cause 2:1 AV block or T-wave alternans. Furthermore, a QTc of 620 ms in a well-appearing neonate with normal feeding is far more consistent with a congenital channelopathy than a transient electrolyte disturbance. Electrolytes should be checked and corrected if abnormal, but this is not the primary diagnosis. (Source: CSANZ: Inherited Cardiac Conditions)

options.text: Congenital long QT syndrome - IV magnesium and beta-blocker
options.rationale: The combination of 2:1 AV block with markedly prolonged QTc (620 ms) and T-wave alternans in a neonate with a structurally normal heart and negative maternal autoantibodies is diagnostic of congenital long QT syndrome presenting with functional 2:1 AV block (the QT interval is so long that every other P wave falls during the refractory period). This is a high-risk presentation with mortality exceeding 50% in the first year without treatment. Immediate management includes IV magnesium sulfate to stabilise the myocardium, followed by initiation of a beta-blocker (propranolol or nadolol). Temporary pacing may be needed. Genetic testing for KCNQ1, KCNH2, and SCN5A mutations guides long-term management. (Source: CSANZ: Inherited Cardiac Conditions; Heart Rhythm Society: LQTS Guidelines)

options.text: Congenital complete heart block - urgent pacemaker insertion
options.rationale: While this neonate has a ventricular rate of 48 bpm which typically warrants consideration of pacing, the underlying diagnosis is critical. The markedly prolonged QTc and T-wave alternans indicate that the 2:1 AV block is functional (related to the extremely long repolarisation period in LQTS) rather than structural conduction disease. Pacing without addressing the underlying channelopathy (with magnesium and beta-blockade) risks triggering torsades de pointes. (Source: CSANZ: Inherited Cardiac Conditions)

explanation.summary: Congenital long QT syndrome presenting as 2:1 AV block in a neonate is a rare but life-threatening presentation. The mechanism is functional rather than structural: the QT interval is so prolonged that every other atrial impulse arrives during the ventricular refractory period, creating pseudo-2:1 block. T-wave alternans on the neonatal ECG is a hallmark sign and indicates high arrhythmic risk. This presentation carries greater than 50% mortality in the first year without treatment. Genetic testing identifies the channel mutation (KCNQ1 in LQT1, KCNH2 in LQT2, SCN5A in LQT3), which guides genotype-specific management.
key_points: 2:1 AV block with markedly prolonged QTc in a neonate is functional LQTS, not structural heart block
T-wave alternans is a hallmark ECG sign indicating high torsades de pointes risk
IV magnesium sulfate is the immediate stabilisation measure, followed by beta-blocker initiation
Mortality exceeds 50% in the first year without treatment for neonatal LQTS presenting as 2:1 block
Genetic testing for KCNQ1 (LQT1), KCNH2 (LQT2), and SCN5A (LQT3) guides genotype-specific therapy
explanation.pearls: Neonatal 2:1 AV block with a QTc above 600 ms is almost always functional LQTS, not structural conduction disease - the QT interval is so long that every second P wave falls in the ventricular refractory period, and beta-blockade (not pacing alone) is the life-saving treatment.

subtopic_detail: MCAD deficiency - hypoketotic hypoglycaemia
stem: A 10-month-old boy presents to the emergency department after being found obtunded by his parents at 6 am. He has had a viral gastroenteritis for the past 2 days with poor oral intake and vomiting. He was well prior to this illness. His newborn screening result is documented as normal in his health record but on review was performed before the expanded metabolic screen was available at his birth hospital. He has one older sibling who is healthy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 170, RR 40, SpO2 98%, Temp 37.2 C, GCS 9 (E2V3M4), capillary refill 3 seconds
data_table.Examination: Obtunded. Mild hepatomegaly (liver 3 cm below costal margin). No rash. No focal neurological signs.
data_table.Investigations: BSL 1.2 mmol/L. Blood ketones 0.3 mmol/L (inappropriately low for this degree of hypoglycaemia). VBG: pH 7.25, pCO2 35 mmHg, HCO3 16 mmol/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L. Ammonia 95 micromol/L (mildly elevated). ALT 180 U/L, AST 210 U/L. CK 450 U/L (mildly elevated). Urine: no ketones detected.

lead_in: After immediate IV dextrose resuscitation, what is the most likely underlying diagnosis?

options.text: Glycogen storage disease type I with fasting intolerance
options.rationale: GSD type I (von Gierke disease) causes fasting hypoglycaemia but with significantly elevated lactate (often above 5 mmol/L), marked hepatomegaly from glycogen accumulation, and lactic acidosis. The hallmark is hypoglycaemia WITH elevated lactate. While this child has mild lactic acidosis, the key discriminating feature is the absence of ketones - GSD type I produces hypoketotic hypoglycaemia in some contexts, but the very low ketones with hepatomegaly and elevated CK points more strongly to a fatty acid oxidation defect. (Source: RACP: Australasian Society for Inborn Errors of Metabolism; GeneReviews: MCAD)
source_refs: RACP: Australasian Society for Inborn Errors of Metabolism

options.text: Medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency
options.rationale: MCAD deficiency is the most common fatty acid oxidation disorder (prevalence approximately 1 in 10,000). The classic presentation is hypoketotic hypoglycaemia during fasting stress (often precipitated by intercurrent illness with poor intake). The inappropriate absence of ketones (blood ketones less than 0.5 mmol/L with BSL less than 2.5 mmol/L) is the hallmark - the body cannot oxidise medium-chain fatty acids for ketogenesis. Hepatomegaly and elevated transaminases occur from hepatic fatty infiltration. The mildly elevated CK reflects impaired skeletal muscle fatty acid oxidation. This child's newborn screen predated expanded metabolic screening, which now includes MCAD detection via C8-acylcarnitine on the Guthrie card. (Source: RACP: Australasian Society for Inborn Errors of Metabolism; GeneReviews: MCAD Deficiency)
source_refs: RACP: Australasian Society for Inborn Errors of Metabolism

options.text: Insulinoma causing persistent hyperinsulinaemic hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Hyperinsulinism causes hypoketotic hypoglycaemia but presents with recurrent hypoglycaemia without fasting stress, typically in infancy. Insulinomas are extremely rare in children. The elevated transaminases, CK, and hepatomegaly in the context of an intercurrent illness with poor oral intake are much more consistent with a fatty acid oxidation defect than hyperinsulinism. (Source: GeneReviews: MCAD Deficiency)

options.text: Adrenal insufficiency with cortisol deficiency during illness
options.rationale: Adrenal insufficiency can cause hypoglycaemia during illness, but does not characteristically produce hypoketotic hypoglycaemia with elevated transaminases and CK. In cortisol deficiency, the expected response is ketotic hypoglycaemia. Additionally, adrenal insufficiency typically presents with electrolyte abnormalities (hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia). (Source: RACP: Australasian Society for Inborn Errors of Metabolism)
source_refs: RACP: Australasian Society for Inborn Errors of Metabolism

options.text: Reye syndrome from recent aspirin use during viral illness
options.rationale: Reye syndrome presents with encephalopathy and hepatic dysfunction after viral illness with aspirin exposure. It is now extremely rare following public health campaigns against aspirin use in children. This child has no documented aspirin exposure, and the hypoketotic hypoglycaemia pattern with mild CK elevation is more consistent with an underlying metabolic disorder. (Source: GeneReviews: MCAD Deficiency)

explanation.summary: MCAD deficiency is the most common fatty acid oxidation disorder, now detected on newborn metabolic screening via elevated C8-acylcarnitine (octanoylcarnitine) on the Guthrie card. Children born before expanded screening was universally available may present with their first metabolic crisis during an intercurrent illness with poor oral intake. The hallmark is hypoketotic hypoglycaemia - the body cannot generate ketone bodies from medium-chain fatty acids during fasting. Hepatic fatty infiltration causes hepatomegaly and transaminitis. The acute management is IV dextrose resuscitation and avoidance of fasting. Long-term management involves emergency protocols, carnitine supplementation, and avoidance of prolonged fasting.
key_points: MCAD deficiency is the most common fatty acid oxidation disorder (1 in 10,000)
Hallmark: hypoketotic hypoglycaemia (blood ketones inappropriately low for the degree of hypoglycaemia)
Precipitated by fasting stress during intercurrent illness with poor oral intake
Detected on expanded newborn metabolic screen via elevated C8-acylcarnitine (octanoylcarnitine)
The A985G mutation in ACADM accounts for approximately 80% of cases in populations of northern European descent
explanation.pearls: The critical diagnostic clue in MCAD deficiency is the discordance between the BSL and the ketone level - a BSL below 2.5 mmol/L with blood ketones below 0.5 mmol/L (or absent urine ketones) indicates a fatty acid oxidation defect and should trigger an acylcarnitine profile and urine organic acids before recovery.

sources.label: RACP: Australasian Society for Inborn Errors of Metabolism

subtopic_detail: Macrophage activation syndrome complicating systemic JIA
stem: An 8-year-old girl with known systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (sJIA) presents with high spiking fevers to 41 C that have become persistent rather than quotidian over the past 3 days. She has developed progressive confusion, bruising, and abdominal distension. Her sJIA has been managed with anakinra but she recently had a dose reduction. Her parents describe a 'different kind of sick' compared with her usual flares.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 150, BP 90/55 mmHg, RR 32, Temp 40.8 C, SpO2 95%
data_table.Examination: Confused, GCS 13 (E3V4M6). Jaundiced. Hepatosplenomegaly. Scattered petechiae and bruising. Lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 2.8 x10^9/L (was 22 x10^9/L last week), Hb 88 g/L, platelets 45 x10^9/L. Ferritin 28,500 microg/L (was 800 microg/L last week). LDH 2,450 U/L. Triglycerides 5.8 mmol/L. Fibrinogen 0.9 g/L (low). AST 380 U/L, ALT 290 U/L. D-dimer 8.5 mg/L. ESR 8 mm/hr (paradoxically low).

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Increase anakinra dose with addition of IV immunoglobulin
options.rationale: While IL-1 blockade (anakinra) is used in sJIA-MAS, increasing the dose alone is insufficient for established MAS with organ dysfunction. This child has life-threatening MAS with encephalopathy, coagulopathy, and cytopenias. High-dose corticosteroids are the first-line treatment for MAS. Anakinra dose escalation may be appropriate as adjunctive therapy but not as the primary intervention. (Source: PRINTO/PRES: MAS Classification Criteria; Ravelli et al, Ann Rheum Dis 2016)

options.text: Empiric IV antibiotics and blood cultures for presumed sepsis
options.rationale: While sepsis can mimic or trigger MAS and blood cultures should be drawn, the clinical picture (paradoxically falling ESR, soaring ferritin, falling fibrinogen, falling counts from previously elevated levels, hepatosplenomegaly in a child with sJIA) is characteristic of MAS. Treatment must target the immune dysregulation, not just infection. Antibiotics alone will not address the cytokine storm. (Source: PRINTO/PRES: MAS Classification Criteria)

options.text: IV methylprednisolone pulse and cyclosporin with ICU admission
options.rationale: This child has fulminant macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) complicating sJIA, evidenced by the paradoxical fall in ESR and WCC (from previously high levels), massively elevated ferritin (above 10,000 microg/L), falling fibrinogen (DIC), encephalopathy, cytopenias, and organ dysfunction. First-line treatment is high-dose IV methylprednisolone (30 mg/kg, max 1 g, daily for 3 days) followed by oral dexamethasone. Cyclosporin A is added as second-line or concurrently in severe cases. ICU admission is essential for haemodynamic monitoring and management of coagulopathy. Anakinra dose escalation may be added. (Source: PRINTO/PRES: MAS Classification Criteria; ACR/EULAR sJIA-MAS Treatment Recommendations)

options.text: Fresh frozen plasma and platelet transfusion with observation
options.rationale: Blood product support (FFP, cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen, platelets) is important supportive care for the consumptive coagulopathy, but does not address the underlying immune dysregulation driving MAS. Without immunosuppressive treatment, the cytokine storm will continue consuming blood products. Observation without immunosuppression risks rapid deterioration and death. (Source: PRINTO/PRES: MAS Classification Criteria)

options.text: Etoposide-based chemotherapy per HLH-2004 protocol
options.rationale: The HLH-2004 protocol (etoposide, dexamethasone, cyclosporin) is used for primary (genetic) haemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis. MAS complicating sJIA is a secondary form that typically responds to corticosteroids and cyclosporin without the need for etoposide. Etoposide is reserved for refractory MAS not responding to first-line immunosuppression. (Source: ACR/EULAR sJIA-MAS Treatment Recommendations)

explanation.summary: Macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) is a life-threatening complication of systemic JIA, occurring in 10-15% of sJIA patients. It is characterised by uncontrolled macrophage and T-cell activation with a cytokine storm. The key diagnostic clues are paradoxical changes: falling ESR (from fibrinogen consumption), falling WCC and platelets (from previously elevated levels in sJIA), and massively rising ferritin. MAS shares criteria with secondary haemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH). Treatment is high-dose corticosteroids with cyclosporin as second-line. Mortality without treatment approaches 20-30%.
key_points: Paradoxically falling ESR in active sJIA is a red flag for MAS (ESR falls because fibrinogen is consumed)
Ferritin above 10,000 microg/L in sJIA is highly suggestive of MAS
Falling WCC and platelets FROM previously elevated levels (not just low counts) is characteristic
First-line: IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg (max 1 g) daily for 3 days; second-line: cyclosporin A
Anakinra dose escalation (up to 10 mg/kg/day) is increasingly used as adjunctive therapy in sJIA-MAS
explanation.pearls: The most specific early warning sign of MAS in sJIA is a paradoxical fall in ESR while ferritin rises - this discordance occurs because fibrinogen (the main determinant of ESR) is consumed by DIC while ferritin is released by activated macrophages.

subtopic_detail: Leigh syndrome - mitochondrial disorder presentation
stem: A 14-month-old boy presents with a 3-month history of developmental regression. He had been walking with support but has lost this skill and now has truncal hypotonia and can no longer sit unsupported. His parents describe episodic vomiting and lethargy, often during minor illnesses. He was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy. Family history reveals a maternal uncle who died at age 3 from an undiagnosed neurological condition.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130, RR 28, SpO2 98%, Temp 36.7 C
data_table.Examination: Truncal hypotonia with appendicular spasticity. Nystagmus. Absent deep tendon reflexes. Intermittent dystonic posturing of the upper limbs. Growth parameters: weight 9.2 kg (10th centile, was 25th), length 75 cm (25th centile).
data_table.Investigations: Venous lactate 5.8 mmol/L (elevated). Pyruvate 0.08 mmol/L. Lactate-to-pyruvate ratio 72 (elevated, normal < 20). Plasma alanine elevated. CSF lactate 4.2 mmol/L (elevated, normal < 2.0 mmol/L). Urine organic acids: elevated lactate and Krebs cycle intermediates.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the suspected diagnosis?

options.text: Whole exome sequencing including mitochondrial DNA analysis
options.rationale: Genetic testing is the ultimate confirmatory investigation for mitochondrial disease but requires targeted analysis informed by the clinical and imaging phenotype. While whole exome sequencing will eventually be performed, MRI brain should be obtained first as the characteristic Leigh syndrome pattern (bilateral symmetric basal ganglia and brainstem lesions) narrows the differential and guides genetic testing priorities. (Source: RCPCH: Mitochondrial Disease; GeneReviews: Leigh Syndrome)

options.text: Muscle biopsy with respiratory chain enzyme analysis
options.rationale: Muscle biopsy with mitochondrial respiratory chain enzyme analysis was historically the gold standard for diagnosing mitochondrial disorders. However, it is invasive, may show normal results in some mitochondrial diseases (especially mtDNA mutations with tissue mosaicism), and has been largely superseded by genetic testing as the primary diagnostic tool. It remains useful when genetic testing is inconclusive. (Source: RCPCH: Mitochondrial Disease)

options.text: Skin fibroblast culture for respiratory chain enzyme assay
options.rationale: Fibroblast culture is less invasive than muscle biopsy but requires 4-6 weeks for cell growth and has lower sensitivity than muscle tissue. It is not the first-line investigation for a child with a classic clinical presentation and biochemical profile. Imaging should precede invasive tissue testing. (Source: GeneReviews: Leigh Syndrome)

options.text: Electroencephalogram with prolonged monitoring for epilepsy
options.rationale: While seizures occur in mitochondrial disease, EEG is not the diagnostic investigation. This child's presentation is one of developmental regression with movement disorder and metabolic derangement, not primarily epilepsy. EEG may be useful for seizure characterisation but does not confirm the underlying mitochondrial diagnosis. (Source: RCPCH: Mitochondrial Disease)

options.text: MRI brain with spectroscopy for characteristic lesion pattern
options.rationale: MRI brain is the most appropriate next investigation. Leigh syndrome characteristically shows bilateral symmetric T2-hyperintense lesions in the basal ganglia (putamen and caudate), thalami, and brainstem (periaqueductal grey, substantia nigra). MR spectroscopy may show an elevated lactate peak within the lesions. This pattern, combined with the clinical features (developmental regression, movement disorder, nystagmus), elevated blood and CSF lactate with a high lactate-to-pyruvate ratio, and maternal inheritance pattern, makes a clinical diagnosis of Leigh syndrome. The imaging pattern guides subsequent genetic testing. (Source: RCPCH: Mitochondrial Disease; GeneReviews: Leigh Syndrome)

explanation.summary: Leigh syndrome (subacute necrotising encephalomyelopathy) is the most common paediatric mitochondrial disorder, caused by defects in mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation. It presents with developmental regression, movement disorders, and brainstem dysfunction in infancy or early childhood. The hallmark is bilateral symmetric basal ganglia and brainstem lesions on MRI. The elevated lactate-to-pyruvate ratio above 20 indicates impaired oxidative phosphorylation. Maternal inheritance (through a maternal uncle) suggests a mitochondrial DNA mutation. Over 75 nuclear and mitochondrial genes have been implicated. There is no curative treatment; management is supportive with cofactor supplementation.
key_points: Leigh syndrome shows bilateral symmetric T2-hyperintense lesions in the basal ganglia and brainstem on MRI
Elevated lactate-to-pyruvate ratio above 20 indicates impaired mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation
CSF lactate is often elevated even when blood lactate is normal, reflecting CNS metabolic dysfunction
Maternal inheritance pattern suggests mitochondrial DNA mutation (maternally inherited through mothers to all children)
Episodic metabolic decompensation during intercurrent illness is characteristic of mitochondrial disease
explanation.pearls: In Leigh syndrome, the lactate-to-pyruvate ratio above 20 distinguishes mitochondrial respiratory chain defects (elevated ratio) from pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency (normal or low ratio) - both cause lactic acidosis and basal ganglia lesions, but the treatment implications differ (ketogenic diet for PDH deficiency, cofactor supplementation for respiratory chain defects).
